full_prompt: Design a research project on mechanochemical synthesis of pharmaceutical co-crystals using benign milling conditions. Return idea_plan output.
chosen:
Rationale: Start broad to identify established mechanochemical cocrystal methods and gaps specifically tied to benign milling conditions, moisture/solvent minimization, and pharmaceutical relevance.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"mechanochemical synthesis pharmaceutical co-crystals benign milling liquid assisted grinding humidity low energy review","discipline":"chemistry","max_results":5}
```  
[result]: Found candidates including Friščić & Jones 2009, DOI `10.1021/cg800764n`, on mechanisms of cocrystal formation by grinding; Braga, Maini & Grepioni 2013, DOI `10.1039/C3CS60014A`, on mechanochemical cocrystal preparation; Hasa & Jones 2017, DOI `10.1016/j.addr.2017.05.001`, on pharmaceutical solid-form screening by mechanochemistry; and James et al. 2012, DOI `10.1039/C1CS15171A`, on cleaner mechanochemical synthesis.  

Rationale: Read the most relevant mechanistic and pharmaceutical-practice papers to ground the project in known mechanisms and practical limitations.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1021/cg800764n","10.1039/C3CS60014A","10.1016/j.addr.2017.05.001"],"focus":["mechanism","benign conditions","pharmaceutical cocrystal screening","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: The papers establish neat grinding and liquid-assisted grinding as effective, greener routes to cocrystals, with mechanisms often involving eutectic formation, amorphization, surface diffusion, or transient solvated phases. Key limitations are empirical solvent/drop selection, risk of overmilling-induced amorphization or polymorph changes, poor comparability across mills, and limited real-time control under very mild conditions.  

Rationale: Extract a concise research genome from the literature to identify a tractable delta rather than merely proposing another cocrystal screen.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.1021/cg800764n","10.1039/C3CS60014A","10.1016/j.addr.2017.05.001"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: `mechanism_genome`: mechanochemical activation plus small amounts of benign liquid or humidity accelerates molecular diffusion and synthon formation. `niche_genome`: pharmaceutical APIs and GRAS coformers under neat, liquid-assisted, or ion/liquid-assisted grinding. `observation_genome`: tiny liquid quantities can switch rate, phase outcome, and polymorph selectivity. `limitation_genome`: solvent choice and milling energy are usually optimized empirically, with limited in situ feedback. `delta_genome`: combine benign micro-liquid/humidity dosing with low-energy milling and real-time Raman/PXRD monitoring. `claim_genome`: controlled, low-intensity mechanochemistry can produce target cocrystals reproducibly with lower solvent and energy burden.  

Rationale: Check whether the proposed combination—humidity/micro-LAG dose control, deliberately low milling energy, and adaptive in situ monitoring for pharmaceutical cocrystals—is sufficiently differentiated.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Closed-loop benign mechanochemical synthesis of pharmaceutical cocrystals using low-frequency/low-ball-mass milling, GRAS micro-liquid or humidity dosing, and in situ Raman/PXRD to stop at complete cocrystal formation while avoiding overmilling amorphization.","against_paper_ids":["10.1021/cg800764n","10.1039/C3CS60014A","10.1016/j.addr.2017.05.001","10.1039/C1CS15171A"]}
```  
[result]: Related elements exist separately—LAG, neat grinding, humidity effects, and in situ mechanochemical monitoring—but the explicit closed-loop design for benign-condition pharmaceutical cocrystal synthesis with quantitative green metrics and overmilling avoidance appears underexplored. Novelty is strongest if the project compares open-loop empirical milling versus adaptive stopping across multiple API/coformer pairs.  

Rationale: Propose a focused project with a clear experimental matrix, benign-condition constraints, and measurable green/process outcomes.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","idea_plan":{"Idea":"Develop a closed-loop, benign mechanochemical platform for synthesizing pharmaceutical co-crystals under low-energy milling using GRAS micro-liquid or humidity-assisted conditions, with in situ spectroscopic feedback to maximize cocrystal yield while minimizing solvent, energy input, and overmilling-induced amorphization.","ImplementationSteps":{"1_select_model_systems":"Choose 4–6 pharmaceutically relevant API/coformer pairs with known supramolecular synthons and different hydrogen-bonding motifs, e.g., caffeine–glutaric acid, carbamazepine–saccharin, nicotinamide-based systems, and ibuprofen–isonicotinamide or related GRAS-compatible coformers.","2_define_benign_condition_space":"Restrict milling to mild frequencies, reduced ball mass, ambient temperature, and benign additives only: water, ethanol, ethyl acetate, or controlled relative humidity. Use liquid-to-solid ratios in the micro-LAG range and include neat grinding as a baseline.","3_closed_loop_monitoring":"Integrate in situ Raman spectroscopy, periodic PXRD, and temperature logging to track disappearance of API/coformer peaks, cocrystal peak growth, transient amorphous content, and local heating. Implement an adaptive stopping rule when the cocrystal signal plateaus or amorphous background begins to rise.","4_mechanistic_mapping":"Construct phase/time maps as a function of milling energy, humidity, liquid identity, and liquid amount. Test whether benign liquids act mainly through eutectic formation, surface wetting, transient solution mediation, or plasticization by comparing kinetics with hygroscopicity, DSC eutectic behavior, and post-milling microscopy.","5_scale_and_stability_check":"Scale the best recipes from small jars to gram-scale mild milling, then assess solid-form purity, residual solvent, short-term humidity stability, and dissolution-rate improvement relative to the parent API and solution-grown reference cocrystals."},"ImplementationOrder":["1_select_model_systems","2_define_benign_condition_space","3_closed_loop_monitoring","4_mechanistic_mapping","5_scale_and_stability_check"],"Dataset":"Experimental dataset comprising time-resolved Raman spectra, PXRD patterns, milling frequency/ball-mass/time profiles, liquid-to-solid ratio, relative humidity, jar temperature, conversion versus time, isolated yield, amorphous fraction estimates, residual solvent/water content, DSC/TGA traces, microscopy images, and dissolution/stability measurements for each API/coformer pair.","EvaluationMetrics":{"CocrystalPerformance":["PXRD-confirmed phase purity ≥95%","isolated yield ≥90% where isolation is required","Raman-derived conversion time relative to neat grinding","amorphous content below predefined threshold, e.g., <5–10%"],"BenignProcessMetrics":["solvent amount in µL per mg solid","E-factor or process mass intensity excluding reusable milling media","specific energy proxy from milling frequency, time, and ball mass","maximum jar temperature rise","percentage of trials using water/ethanol/humidity rather than less benign liquids"],"RobustnessMetrics":["reproducibility across triplicate mills","successful transfer from screening scale to gram scale","solid-form stability after storage at selected RH values","residual solvent/water within acceptable pharmaceutical limits"],"PharmaceuticalMetrics":["intrinsic or powder dissolution-rate improvement versus parent API","maintenance of target cocrystal form after dissolution/stability testing","comparison to solution-crystallized reference where available"]},"ExpectedOutcome":"The project should deliver a generalizable low-energy mechanochemical workflow that replaces empirical overmilling with feedback-controlled benign synthesis. Expected outputs are phase-pure pharmaceutical cocrystals obtained with minimal liquid and energy input, mechanistic rules linking humidity/micro-liquid choice to transformation pathways, and a practical decision map for selecting neat, humidity-assisted, or GRAS-LAG conditions for future pharmaceutical cocrystal development."}}
```  
[result]: Proposed a chemistry research project centered on adaptive, low-impact mechanochemical synthesis of pharmaceutical co-crystals with explicit implementation steps, dataset, evaluation metrics, and expected outcomes.

full_prompt: Outline a strategy for studying microplastic adsorption of common pesticides in freshwater systems. Please provide idea_plan output.
chosen:
Rationale: First identify relevant chemistry literature on sorption of organic contaminants/pesticides to microplastics and freshwater-relevant factors, so the strategy is anchored in prior methods and gaps.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "microplastic adsorption pesticides freshwater sorption kinetics isotherms atrazine chlorpyrifos polyethylene polystyrene",
  "discipline": "chemistry",
  "max_results": 5
}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers include Hüffer & Hofmann 2016 (`10.1016/j.envpol.2016.06.037`) on sorption of organic compounds to microplastics, Bakir et al. 2012 (`10.1016/j.marpolbul.2012.12.010`) on sorption/desorption of persistent organic pollutants to microplastics, and Velzeboer et al. 2014 (`10.1021/es405721v`) on strong sorption to nano/microplastics. Results suggest robust batch sorption methodology but fewer freshwater pesticide studies combining aging, biofilms, and realistic water matrices.  

Rationale: Read the most methodologically useful papers to extract mechanisms, experimental niches, observations, and limitations for designing a targeted pesticide study.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_ids": [
    "10.1016/j.envpol.2016.06.037",
    "10.1016/j.marpolbul.2012.12.010",
    "10.1021/es405721v"
  ],
  "focus": [
    "sorption mechanisms",
    "polymer dependence",
    "kinetics and isotherm methods",
    "freshwater matrix variables",
    "limitations for pesticide adsorption"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: The papers support hydrophobic partitioning, surface adsorption, and polymer/aging effects as dominant mechanisms. Common limitations are high contaminant concentrations, simplified matrices, limited pesticide classes, and weak linkage between laboratory partition coefficients and freshwater environmental conditions.  

Rationale: Convert the literature into a compact gene_genome to clarify the study design space and define the contribution.  
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "10.1016/j.envpol.2016.06.037",
    "10.1016/j.marpolbul.2012.12.010",
    "10.1021/es405721v"
  ],
  "schema": [
    "mechanism_genome",
    "niche_genome",
    "observation_genome",
    "limitation_genome",
    "delta_genome",
    "claim_genome"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: mechanism_genome: hydrophobic partitioning, π–π interactions for aromatic pesticides on PS, electrostatic effects for ionizable pesticides, and aging-driven oxidation/biofilm effects. niche_genome: batch sorption/desorption, polymer comparisons, and controlled aqueous chemistry. limitation_genome/delta_genome highlight need for environmentally realistic freshwater matrices, multiple pesticide chemistries, weathered plastics, and transport-relevant validation.  

Rationale: Check that the proposed strategy is not merely a repeat of standard batch adsorption work but adds chemistry-resolved and freshwater-specific novelty.  
```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "idea": "Study adsorption/desorption of common pesticides by virgin, UV-aged, and biofilm-conditioned freshwater microplastics using batch kinetics/isotherms plus flow-through mesocosm validation across realistic pH, dissolved organic carbon, ionic strength, and suspended sediment gradients.",
  "against_paper_ids": [
    "10.1016/j.envpol.2016.06.037",
    "10.1016/j.marpolbul.2012.12.010",
    "10.1021/es405721v"
  ],
  "criteria": [
    "freshwater realism",
    "pesticide diversity",
    "aged and biofilm-coated plastics",
    "transport/desorption validation"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: Novelty is moderate-to-high: individual components exist, but the integrated design across pesticide ionization/hydrophobicity, weathered polymers, natural organic matter, and flow-through freshwater validation is a clear extension beyond typical equilibrium batch studies.  

Rationale: Propose a concrete idea_plan with experimental workflow, dataset design, metrics, and expected chemical insight.  
```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "idea_plan": {
    "Idea": "Develop a freshwater-relevant adsorption/desorption framework for common pesticides on microplastics by combining controlled batch chemistry with weathered-polymer and flow-through validation. The study will quantify how polymer type, particle aging, pesticide physicochemical class, dissolved organic matter, pH, ionic strength, and suspended sediment alter pesticide partitioning and remobilization.",
    "ImplementationSteps": {
      "1_literature_and_target_selection": "Select representative pesticides spanning hydrophobic neutral, aromatic, polar, and ionizable chemistries: atrazine, chlorpyrifos, imidacloprid, 2,4-D, glyphosate or metolachlor. Select common freshwater microplastics: polyethylene, polypropylene, polystyrene, PET, and PVC in controlled size fractions, e.g., 20-100 um and 100-500 um.",
      "2_material_characterization": "Characterize virgin, UV-aged, mechanically abraded, and biofilm-conditioned microplastics by FTIR or Raman spectroscopy, contact angle, BET or dye-accessible surface area, zeta potential, SEM, particle-size analysis, and carbonyl index for oxidation state.",
      "3_batch_kinetics": "Run dark, temperature-controlled batch adsorption experiments at environmentally relevant pesticide concentrations plus higher analytical calibration levels. Sample time points from minutes to days and fit pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order, intraparticle diffusion, and film-diffusion models.",
      "4_equilibrium_isotherms": "Measure adsorption over concentration gradients and fit linear Kd, Freundlich, and Langmuir models. Compare normalized coefficients by polymer mass, surface area, and organic-carbon-equivalent content.",
      "5_freshwater_matrix_tests": "Repeat key conditions across pH 5.5-8.5, Ca/Mg ionic strength, natural dissolved organic carbon, humic acid, nitrate/phosphate, and suspended sediment. Include single-pesticide and mixed-pesticide treatments to detect competitive adsorption.",
      "6_desorption_and_remobilization": "Transfer pesticide-loaded microplastics into clean synthetic freshwater and natural water to quantify hysteresis, desorption half-lives, and pulse release under changing DOC, pH, and sediment conditions.",
      "7_flow_through_validation": "Use small columns or recirculating mesocosms containing natural freshwater, sediment, and microplastics to test whether batch-derived partition coefficients predict pesticide retention and transport under realistic hydrodynamics.",
      "8_analysis_and_quality_control": "Quantify pesticides by LC-MS/MS for polar compounds and GC-MS/MS for hydrophobic pesticides such as chlorpyrifos where appropriate. Include procedural blanks, plastic-free controls, pesticide-only controls, mass-balance checks, isotope-labeled surrogates, and contamination-controlled glassware workflows.",
      "9_modeling_and_interpretation": "Build regression or mechanistic models linking adsorption to pesticide logKow, pKa, water solubility, polymer crystallinity, surface oxidation, zeta potential, DOC, and ionic strength. Use sensitivity analysis to identify dominant freshwater controls."
    },
    "ImplementationOrder": [
      "1_literature_and_target_selection",
      "2_material_characterization",
      "3_batch_kinetics",
      "4_equilibrium_isotherms",
      "5_freshwater_matrix_tests",
      "6_desorption_and_remobilization",
      "7_flow_through_validation",
      "8_analysis_and_quality_control",
      "9_modeling_and_interpretation"
    ],
    "Dataset": "A structured experimental dataset with rows defined by pesticide, polymer type, particle-size class, aging state, biofilm state, concentration, contact time, pH, ionic strength, DOC, sediment concentration, temperature, and replicate. Response fields include aqueous pesticide concentration, microplastic-associated concentration, mass balance, adsorption percent, desorption percent, kinetic constants, Kd, Koc-equivalent, Freundlich parameters, Langmuir parameters, hysteresis index, and analytical uncertainty.",
    "EvaluationMetrics": {
      "adsorption_capacity": "Kd, surface-area-normalized Kd, Freundlich Kf and n, and Langmuir qmax where applicable.",
      "kinetics": "time to apparent equilibrium, pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order rate constants, intraparticle diffusion fit quality.",
      "desorption": "desorption fraction, desorption half-life, hysteresis index, and remobilized mass under water-chemistry shifts.",
      "matrix_effects": "fold-change in Kd or Kf caused by DOC, pH, ionic strength, sediment, aging, and biofilm conditioning.",
      "model_quality": "R2, RMSE, AIC/BIC for isotherm and kinetic models; cross-validated error for predictive models.",
      "environmental_relevance": "comparison of observed partitioning at realistic pesticide and microplastic concentrations against high-dose laboratory conditions.",
      "quality_control": "mass-balance recovery, blank contamination rate, surrogate recovery, replicate relative standard deviation."
    },
    "ExpectedOutcome": "The study should identify which pesticide-polymer combinations create meaningful adsorption reservoirs in freshwater and which are dominated by water or sediment partitioning. Expected trends are stronger adsorption for hydrophobic pesticides such as chlorpyrifos on PE/PP and enhanced aromatic interactions for compounds such as atrazine on PS, while highly polar or ionized pesticides should show weaker and more pH-dependent binding. UV aging and biofilms may either increase adsorption through oxygenated functional groups and biofilm organic matter or decrease hydrophobic partitioning through surface masking. The final output will be a validated decision framework for predicting when microplastics act as pesticide vectors versus minor sorbents in freshwater systems."
  }
}
```  
[result]: The proposed idea_plan is chemistry-focused, experimentally feasible, and explicitly links adsorption mechanisms to freshwater conditions and analytical validation. It provides a complete workflow from controlled sorption chemistry to environmental interpretation.

full_prompt: Create a plan to evaluate polymer electrolyte membranes for safer sodium-ion batteries. Please use idea_plan output.
chosen:
Rationale: I first search for recent sodium-battery polymer/gel/solid electrolyte membrane papers plus safety-oriented reviews, so the evaluation plan is anchored in known failure modes: flammability, Na dendrites, interfacial instability, and poor room-temperature conductivity.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"polymer electrolyte membrane sodium-ion battery safety gel solid polymer electrolyte flammability dendrite review","discipline":"chemistry","k":5}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers included: DOI:10.1039/C6CS00776G (Hwang/Myung/Sun, *Chem. Soc. Rev.*, sodium-ion battery review), DOI:10.1021/acs.chemrev.9b00427 (solid-state sodium battery electrolytes review), DOI:10.1016/j.jpowsour.2014.12.104 (polymer electrolytes for sodium batteries), DOI:10.1039/C8TA08960E (Na solid/polymer electrolyte interface discussion), and DOI:10.1016/j.ensm.2018.07.004 (gel/polymer electrolyte safety concepts).

Rationale: I read the most directly relevant polymer-electrolyte and solid-state sodium-electrolyte sources to extract design variables and evaluation endpoints rather than proposing only electrochemical cycling.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1016/j.jpowsour.2014.12.104","10.1021/acs.chemrev.9b00427","10.1039/C8TA08960E"],"focus":["polymer matrix","Na salt","ionic conductivity","transference number","interfacial resistance","safety tests"]}
```  
[result]: The papers emphasize that PEO-like membranes often need elevated temperature for useful conductivity, gel PVDF-HFP/PMMA systems improve room-temperature conductivity but can retain flammable solvent, and ceramic/polymer hybrids can improve modulus and Na interfacial stability. Common gaps are inconsistent reporting of Na⁺ transference number, abuse tolerance, gas/heat release, and membrane mechanical puncture resistance.

Rationale: I convert the literature into a compact genome to distinguish what has been evaluated from what the new plan must add.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.1016/j.jpowsour.2014.12.104","10.1021/acs.chemrev.9b00427","10.1039/C8TA08960E"],"schema":"gene_genome"}
```  
[result]:  
```json
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Na+ transport through solvating polymer segments or gelled liquid domains; ceramic fillers and crosslinking can raise modulus, suppress dendrite penetration, and stabilize interphases.",
  "niche_genome": "Safer sodium-ion/sodium-metal cells using free-standing polymer electrolyte membranes between hard-carbon or Na-metal anodes and layered oxide or Prussian-blue cathodes.",
  "observation_genome": "Higher salt dissociation and plasticization improve conductivity; ceramic/polymer composites improve dimensional stability; interfacial impedance and low-temperature conductivity remain limiting.",
  "limitation_genome": "Many studies optimize conductivity/cycling but under-report flame resistance, thermal runaway proxies, puncture strength, gas evolution, and standardized Na+ transference number.",
  "delta_genome": "Need a benchmark protocol that ranks membranes by coupled electrochemical, mechanical, thermal, and abuse-safety metrics in identical sodium-ion cell formats.",
  "claim_genome": "A multidimensional safety-performance score can identify polymer electrolyte membranes that are safer without sacrificing sodium-ion battery rate capability and cycle life."
}
```

Rationale: I check whether the plan’s novelty is in membrane chemistry synthesis or in the evaluation protocol; the stronger contribution is a standardized, cross-membrane safety-performance benchmark.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Benchmark PEO, PVDF-HFP gel, PMMA gel, and ceramic-filled crosslinked polymer electrolyte membranes for safer sodium-ion batteries using matched cells and combined electrochemical, thermal, mechanical, flammability, and abuse metrics.","against_paper_ids":["10.1016/j.jpowsour.2014.12.104","10.1021/acs.chemrev.9b00427","10.1039/C6CS00776G"]}
```  
[result]: Similar papers report individual membrane chemistries and standard cycling, but a head-to-head safety benchmark using matched sodium-ion chemistries, Na⁺ transport metrics, calorimetry, puncture, flame tests, and failure-mode scoring appears underdeveloped. Novelty is moderate-to-high as an evaluation framework rather than a new polymer synthesis.

Rationale: I now propose an executable chemistry evaluation plan in the requested `idea_plan` schema, prioritizing comparable membrane fabrication, matched-cell testing, and quantitative safety metrics.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"idea_plan","content":{"Idea":"Develop a standardized evaluation workflow for polymer electrolyte membranes for safer sodium-ion batteries by benchmarking four membrane families—PEO/NaFSI solid polymer, PVDF-HFP carbonate gel, PMMA-based gel, and ceramic-filled crosslinked polymer—under identical sodium-ion cell conditions and ranking them with a combined safety-performance index.","ImplementationSteps":{"1_membrane_preparation":"Prepare free-standing membranes with controlled thickness of 50–100 µm: PEO:NaFSI solid polymer, PVDF-HFP/NaPF6 or NaFSI gel, PMMA/NaFSI gel, and PVDF-HFP or PEO composite membranes containing 5–15 wt% NASICON or beta-alumina filler. full_prompt: Design a research plan for developing colorimetric sensors for detecting phosphate in agricultural runoff. Return idea_plan output.
chosen:

```  
[result]: Candidate literature centers on the phosphomolybdenum blue assay and paper/microfluidic adaptations. Strong anchors include Murphy & Riley’s single-solution phosphate method (DOI: 10.1016/S0003-2670(00)88444-5) and Nagul et al.’s mechanistic review (DOI: 10.1016/j.aca.2015.07.030).  

Rationale: Read the core chemistry sources to extract mechanisms, matrix constraints, and design levers relevant to runoff samples.

    "field-deployable colorimetric phosphate detection",
    "interferences in natural waters",
    "limits of detection and reagent stability"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: Murphy–Riley uses acidic ammonium molybdate/antimony with ascorbic acid reduction to form blue reduced phosphomolybdate. Nagul et al. emphasize that acidity, molybdate concentration, reductant choice, silicate/arsenate interference, and timing strongly affect sensitivity and selectivity.

```

full_prompt: Create an experimental design for comparing antioxidant activity of plant-derived polyphenols under different pH conditions. Provide idea_plan output.
chosen:
Rationale: I first locate established assay/pH literature so the design uses accepted antioxidant readouts while explicitly addressing pH-dependent chemistry rather than treating pH as a nuisance variable.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "plant polyphenols antioxidant activity pH dependence DPPH ABTS FRAP ORAC hydroxyflavones catechins",
  "discipline": "chemistry",
  "max_results": 5
}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates including Lemanska et al. on pH effects in hydroxyflavones (paper_id: `10.1016/S0891-5849(01)00638-4`), Prior/Wu/Schaich on standardized antioxidant assays (paper_id: `10.1021/jf0502698`), and Benzie/Strain on FRAP (paper_id: `10.1006/abio.1996.0292`). These support combining mechanism-sensitive assays with tight pH control.  

Rationale: I read the most directly relevant mechanistic paper to identify design constraints: polyphenol ionization, buffer effects, and assay-specific redox behavior.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_ids": [
    "10.1016/S0891-5849(01)00638-4",
    "10.1021/jf0502698",
    "10.1006/abio.1996.0292"
  ],
  "focus": "pH-dependent antioxidant mechanisms, assay limitations, experimental variables"
}
```  
[result]: Lemanska et al. report that antioxidant behavior of hydroxyflavones changes strongly with pH due to deprotonation and altered electron-transfer capacity. Prior et al. emphasize that different antioxidant assays are not interchangeable; FRAP is intrinsically acidic, while radical-scavenging assays can be adapted but require matrix and solvent controls.  

Rationale: I extract the “genome” of the experimental opportunity to make the design explicit and avoid simply repeating a single-assay comparison.  
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "10.1016/S0891-5849(01)00638-4",
    "10.1021/jf0502698"
  ],
  "schema": "gene_genome"
}
```  
[result]: Mechanism: pH shifts phenolic deprotonation and electron/proton transfer. Niche: plant-derived flavonoids and phenolic acids under food/physiological pH. Observation: antioxidant rankings can invert across assays and pH. Limitation: many studies use one pH or one assay. Delta: factorial pH × compound × assay design with speciation-aware interpretation. Claim: pH-resolved antioxidant profiling gives more meaningful comparisons than single-condition IC50 values.  

Rationale: I check novelty against common antioxidant workflows; the goal is not a new molecule screen, but a reproducible comparative experimental design linking antioxidant response to pH and compound class.  
```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "idea": "factorial comparison of representative plant polyphenols across pH 3, 5, 7.4, and 9 using pH-adapted DPPH/ABTS/ORAC plus FRAP-at-standard-pH control, with LC-UV stability checks and mixed-effects modeling",
  "against_paper_ids": [
    "10.1016/S0891-5849(01)00638-4",
    "10.1021/jf0502698",
    "10.1006/abio.1996.0292"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: The concept is incremental but experimentally valuable: pH effects are known, yet many comparisons lack a balanced compound-class panel, matched buffer controls, degradation checks, and statistical interaction testing. The proposed design is suitable as a rigorous experimental study or methods-comparison protocol.  

Rationale: I now propose the final idea_plan with a concrete factorial chemistry experiment, implementation order, dataset structure, metrics, and expected outcome.

Include Trolox and ascorbic acid as positive controls.",
      "2_pH_conditions": "Prepare matched buffer systems at pH 3.0, 5.0, 7.4, and 9.0 using low-reactivity buffers appropriate to each range, for example citrate/phosphate for acidic conditions, phosphate or HEPES for pH 7.4, and borate or carbonate-free alkaline buffer for pH 9.0. Processing routes include aqueous layer-by-layer assembly, doctor-blading/slip-casting, and controlled drying, but many exemplars prioritize maximum mechanical performance over fast, recyclable packaging manufacture.

full_prompt: Identify ceramic matrix composite architectures that improve fracture toughness without sacrificing oxidation resistance. Provide gene_genome output.
chosen:
Rationale: I first search broadly across CMC toughness mechanisms and oxidation-stable design families, prioritizing review papers that connect architecture to both crack tolerance and environmental durability.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"ceramic matrix composite architecture fracture toughness oxidation resistance oxide oxide SiC SiC interphase self healing review","discipline":"materials","top_k":5}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers include Evans & Zok on brittle-matrix composite mechanics (paper_id: `doi:10.1007/BF00355946`), Naslain on non-oxide CMC design for engines/nuclear applications (paper_id: `doi:10.1016/S0266-3538(03)00230-6`), and Zok on oxide-fiber composites (paper_id: `doi:10.1111/j.1551-2916.2006.01342.x`). The candidates separate three oxidation-compatible routes: oxide/oxide porous matrices, oxidation-shielded SiC/SiC interphases, and self-healing Si-containing matrices.  

Rationale: I read the mechanics and architecture papers to extract which architectural features improve apparent toughness/pseudo-ductility and which avoid the classic carbon-interphase oxidation penalty.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["doi:10.1007/BF00355946","doi:10.1016/S0266-3538(03)00230-6","doi:10.1111/j.1551-2916.2006.01342.x"],"focus":["fiber architecture","matrix porosity","interphase chemistry","oxidation resistance","fracture toughness mechanisms"]}
```  
[result]: Evans & Zok emphasize non-catastrophic fracture from crack deflection, fiber bridging, pullout, and controlled interfacial debonding. Naslain identifies SiC/SiC designs using BN or multilayer interphases plus SiC/self-healing matrices, but notes exposed carbon/BN-rich interfaces are oxidation vulnerabilities. Zok highlights oxide/oxide composites where porous matrices and no-carbon architectures provide damage tolerance while retaining inherent oxidation resistance.  

Rationale: I convert the evidence into mechanism/niche/observation/limitation/delta/claim genes, separating general toughness mechanisms from oxidation-preserving architectural choices.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["doi:10.1007/BF00355946","doi:10.1016/S0266-3538(03)00230-6","doi:10.1111/j.1551-2916.2006.01342.x"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted genes converge on “weak-enough interfaces without oxidizable carbon exposure” as the core design rule. The strongest oxidation-neutral toughness architectures are porous-matrix oxide/oxide CMCs, oxide-compatible crack-deflecting interphases such as monazite-type or porous interfaces, and SiC/SiC with protected/multilayer BN plus dense/self-healing SiC-based matrices.  

Rationale: I check novelty against common CMC designs to avoid recommending architectures that merely trade toughness for poorer oxidation resistance.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claims":["Porous-matrix oxide/oxide CMCs improve damage tolerance without carbon/BN oxidation penalty","Oxidation-stable oxide interphases such as rare-earth phosphate/monazite can provide crack deflection in oxide CMCs","SiC/SiC with multilayer/protected BN and self-healing Si-B-C or SiC matrices mitigates oxidation while retaining fiber pullout"],"baseline":["carbon interphase SiC CMC","single BN interphase SiC CMC","monolithic ceramic","dense brittle oxide matrix composite"]}
```  
[result]: Compared with carbon-coated SiC/SiC, the oxide/oxide porous-matrix and oxide-interphase routes are more oxidation-stable but usually lower-temperature/strength-limited by oxide fibers and matrix creep. Protected BN/self-healing SiC/SiC is less intrinsically oxidation-proof than all-oxide CMCs but better suited to high-temperature structural engine niches when coupled to dense matrices or environmental barrier coatings.  

Rationale: I now synthesize the identified architectures into the requested gene_genome output.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","output_schema":"gene_genome","discipline":"materials","task":"Identify ceramic matrix composite architectures that improve fracture toughness without sacrificing oxidation resistance"}
```  
[result]: Proposed gene genome below.

full_prompt: Explore thermoelectric materials based on earth-abundant elements for low-grade waste-heat recovery. Provide gene_genome output.
chosen:
Rationale: I first search for experimentally validated thermoelectrics using mostly Mg/Cu/Fe/S/Si/Sb/Sn/Se, emphasizing near-room-temperature to ~500 K operation relevant to low-grade waste heat.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"earth abundant thermoelectric materials low grade waste heat Mg3Sb2 tetrahedrite MgAgSb silicide zT near room temperature","discipline":"materials","top_k":6}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers included Mg3Sb2-based Zintl phases (DOI: 10.1002/adma.201602796), tetrahedrite Cu12Sb4S13 (DOI: 10.1002/aenm.201300670), α-MgAgSb (DOI: 10.1073/pnas.1608792113), Mg2Si-based silicides (DOI: 10.1016/j.actamat.2011.06.015), and SnSe (DOI: 10.1038/nature13184). The most relevant low-grade candidates were MgAgSb and Mg3Sb2/Mg3Bi2-family materials, with tetrahedrites as low-cost p-type sulfide alternatives.  

Rationale: I read representative papers to extract mechanisms, temperature windows, and limitations rather than relying only on zT headline values.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1002/adma.201602796","10.1073/pnas.1608792113","10.1002/aenm.201300670","10.1038/nature13184"],"extract":["composition","operating_temperature","transport_mechanism","abundance/toxicity","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: Mg3Sb2-family work highlights defect-controlled n-type conduction and multi-valley bands; α-MgAgSb gives strong low-temperature p-type performance through carrier-scattering/phonon-scattering optimization; tetrahedrites exploit complex sulfide cages and Cu disorder for low lattice thermal conductivity; SnSe shows exceptional anharmonicity but relies on Se and anisotropic single-crystal behavior.  

Rationale: I convert the literature into a materials “genome” so the design space can be compared mechanistically instead of as isolated compounds.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.1002/adma.201602796","10.1073/pnas.1608792113","10.1002/aenm.201300670"],"schema":"gene_genome"}
```  
[result]: Common genes were: intrinsically low lattice thermal conductivity from complex/disordered lattices; band convergence or high valley degeneracy for power factor; controlled point defects/dopants for carrier density; and processing constraints around phase stability, oxidation, and contact compatibility. MgAgSb and Mg3Sb2-family materials best matched the 300–500 K low-grade window.  

Rationale: I check whether the promising direction is merely “use known MgAgSb/Mg3Sb2” or whether there is a defensible delta for new research.

```

The clearest research gap is not a single new compound but an integrated earth-abundant materials genome for manufacturable low-grade modules.

full_prompt: Propose a strategy to design acoustic metamaterials for low-frequency noise reduction in urban buildings. Provide idea_plan output.
chosen:
Rationale: Identify evidence on low-frequency acoustic metamaterials relevant to building envelopes, especially subwavelength resonators, membranes, absorbers, and ventilated silencers.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "low frequency acoustic metamaterial building noise reduction membrane resonator Helmholtz ventilated window facade",
  "discipline": "materials",
  "top_k": 5
}
```

[result]: Found core candidates: Yang et al. PRL 2008 on membrane-type metamaterial attenuation, Mei et al. Nat. Commun. 2012 on low-frequency “dark” acoustic superabsorbers, and Ghaffarivardavagh et al. PRB 2019 on ultra-open ventilated metamaterial silencers. These papers target the key conflict in urban buildings: low-frequency attenuation with thin structures and, ideally, airflow.

Rationale: Read the most relevant mechanisms to ground the design in experimentally demonstrated metamaterial physics.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_ids": [
    "10.1103/PhysRevLett.101.204301",
    "10.1038/ncomms1758",
    "10.1103/PhysRevB.99.024302"
  ]
}
```

[result]: Yang et al. demonstrated membrane-type resonators that create antiresonant transmission loss at low frequencies despite subwavelength thickness. Mei et al. showed near-perfect low-frequency absorption by coupling membrane resonators to dissipative elements. Ghaffarivardavagh et al. demonstrated an open acoustic metamaterial silencer using Fano-like interference, relevant to windows, vents, and façades.

Rationale: Extract a reusable design genome from the literature and identify what must be changed for urban-building deployment.

```action

    "limitation_genome",

full_prompt: Create a research plan for printable flexible piezoelectric materials for human-motion energy harvesting. Provide idea_plan output.
chosen:
Rationale: First, map the main materials strategies for flexible piezoelectric harvesters and identify printable-device precedents, favoring papers with durable mechanical energy harvesting rather than only pressure sensing.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"printable flexible piezoelectric nanogenerator PVDF-TrFE BaTiO3 human motion energy harvesting review inkjet screen printing","discipline":"materials","k":5}
```  
[result]: Candidates cluster around PVDF/P(VDF-TrFE) inks, ceramic-polymer composites, and printed electrodes. Relevant seed papers include Park et al. flexible BaTiO3 nanoparticle composite generators (paper_id: `doi:10.1002/adma.201200105`) and Kim et al. flexible PZT thin-film nanogenerators (paper_id: `doi:10.1038/nnano.2010.279`), plus more recent printable PVDF-TrFE composite reports.  

Rationale: Read representative high-impact baselines to extract mechanisms, bottlenecks, and what is not solved for fully printable human-motion harvesters.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["doi:10.1002/adma.201200105","doi:10.1038/nnano.2010.279"],"fields":["materials","fabrication","device_architecture","performance","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: The BaTiO3-polymer route is mechanically compliant but limited by particle agglomeration, insufficient interfacial stress transfer, and high filler loading. Flexible PZT thin films give high output but rely on non-printable, high-temperature microfabrication and brittle inorganic layers, weakening compatibility with large-area wearable printing.  

Rationale: Convert the literature into a structured genome so the proposed plan targets a clear gap rather than restating known ceramic-polymer composite work.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["doi:10.1002/adma.201200105","doi:10.1038/nnano.2010.279"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: `mechanism_genome`: piezoelectric dipole alignment plus strain transfer from flexible substrate into active phase. `niche_genome`: wearable bending/pressing harvesters. `observation_genome`: high-output ceramics help, but compliant polymers survive deformation. `limitation_genome`: printability, ceramic dispersion, poling voltage, fatigue, and low-frequency human-motion output remain unresolved together. `delta_genome`: need a printable ink architecture that simultaneously raises β-phase/dipole alignment, improves ceramic-polymer coupling, and preserves stretch/flex life. `claim_genome`: interfacially engineered hybrid fillers should outperform simple PVDF/ceramic blends.  

Rationale: Check whether a specific combination—surface-functionalized lead-free piezoceramic + 2D conductive/polar phase + printable fluoropolymer matrix with graded porosity—has enough novelty.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Screen-printable P(VDF-TrFE) ink containing dopamine-silanized BaTiO3 nanowires and low-loading MXene nanosheets, printed as a porous/gradient piezoelectric layer on TPU with printed Ag-nanowire electrodes for knee/elbow human-motion harvesting.","compare_to":["doi:10.1002/adma.201200105","doi:10.1038/nnano.2010.279"],"criteria":["materials_combination","printability","wearable_energy_harvesting","mechanistic_distinction"]}
```  
[result]: Novelty is moderate-to-high if framed around printable interfacial and morphological engineering rather than merely adding fillers. The differentiator is a fully printed lead-free composite where BaTiO3 nanowires improve piezoelectric response, MXene assists β-phase nucleation/local-field concentration at very low loading, and controlled porosity increases compressive strain transfer under slow human motion.  

Rationale: Propose a decisive experimental plan with fabrication, characterization, wearable testing, and success metrics aligned to human-motion harvesting.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","idea_plan":{"Idea":"Develop a fully printable, lead-free, flexible piezoelectric energy harvester based on a porous P(VDF-TrFE)/dopamine-silanized BaTiO3-nanowire/MXene hybrid ink on TPU. The central hypothesis is that interfacial coupling plus low-loading MXene-induced β-phase nucleation will increase effective piezoelectric response, while engineered porosity amplifies strain transfer during low-frequency human motion without sacrificing bend fatigue life.","ImplementationSteps":{"1_InkFormulation":"Synthesize or purchase BaTiO3 nanowires; functionalize them with polydopamine plus silane coupling groups to improve dispersion and stress transfer in P(VDF-TrFE). Prepare MXene flakes at ≤0.5 wt% relative to polymer to avoid electrical percolation. Tune solvent system and rheology for screen or aerosol-jet printing.","2_PrintingArchitecture":"Print Ag-nanowire or PEDOT:PSS/Ag hybrid bottom electrodes on TPU, print the piezoelectric composite layer with sacrificial porogen or phase-separation-induced microporosity, then print top electrodes and encapsulate with thin TPU/PDMS. Compare dense, porous, and filler-gradient active layers.","3_PolingAndOptimization":"Pole devices thermally/electrically below TPU damage limits. Optimize BaTiO3 nanowire loading, MXene loading, porosity fraction, layer thickness, and poling field using a design-of-experiments matrix.","4_MaterialsCharacterization":"Measure β-phase fraction by FTIR/XRD, filler dispersion by SEM/TEM, dielectric constant and loss, leakage current, d33, tensile/bending properties, and interfacial adhesion. Use finite-element modeling to correlate porosity and nanowire orientation with strain localization.","5_HumanMotionDeviceTesting":"Mount devices on knee, elbow, wrist, and shoe insole locations. Test at 0.5–3 Hz bending/compression, record voltage/current/power across load resistances, and demonstrate capacitor charging or powering a low-duty-cycle wearable sensor node."},"ImplementationOrder":["1_InkFormulation","2_PrintingArchitecture","3_PolingAndOptimization","4_MaterialsCharacterization","5_HumanMotionDeviceTesting"],"Dataset":"Experimental dataset containing ink rheology, composition variables, printing parameters, film morphology, β-phase fraction, dielectric/leakage data, d33, mechanical fatigue curves, and device outputs under standardized bending/compression plus real human-motion traces. Baselines: neat printed P(VDF-TrFE), P(VDF-TrFE)/BaTiO3 nanoparticles, P(VDF-TrFE)/BaTiO3 nanowires without MXene, and dense versus porous films.","EvaluationMetrics":{"materials_metrics":["β-phase fraction by FTIR >85% target","effective d33 improvement ≥2x over neat printed P(VDF-TrFE)","dielectric loss tanδ <0.08 at 1 kHz","leakage low enough to maintain stable poling and open-circuit voltage"],"printing_metrics":["continuous films over ≥10 cm2","line/film thickness coefficient of variation <10%","ink pot-life >4 h","nozzle/screen clogging absent over repeated prints"],"energy_harvesting_metrics":["open-circuit voltage, short-circuit current, and areal power density under 1 Hz bending","matched-load power density ≥2x ceramic-polymer baseline at equal strain","capacitor charging rate under knee/elbow motion","stable output after ≥10000 bending cycles"],"wearability_metrics":["bend radius ≤5 mm without cracking","strain tolerance compatible with TPU substrate","output retention ≥80% after sweat/humidity exposure and cyclic bending"]},"ExpectedOutcome":"A printable wearable piezoelectric generator that outperforms simple PVDF/BaTiO3 blends under slow human-motion deformation, with mechanistic evidence linking MXene-assisted β-phase formation, nanowire-polymer interfacial coupling, and porosity-enhanced strain transfer to higher output and better fatigue durability."}}
```  
[result]: Proposed idea_plan is feasible, lead-free, printing-compatible, and addresses the principal literature gap: achieving high low-frequency harvesting output without brittle PZT processing or poorly dispersed high-load ceramic composites.

full_prompt: Investigate whether climate-related financial disclosures reduce firms' cost of capital in emerging markets. Return the output as gene_genome.
chosen:
Rationale: I first search for finance/accounting evidence connecting climate/CSR disclosure to cost of equity/debt, prioritizing papers that can anchor identification and reveal the emerging-market gap.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"climate carbon environmental disclosure cost of capital emerging markets finance accounting cost of equity cost of debt","discipline":"economics_finance","max_results":6}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers include Dhaliwal et al. 2011 (paper_id: `doi:10.2308/accr.00000005`) on CSR reporting and cost of equity, Matsumura et al. 2014 (`doi:10.2308/accr-50629`) on carbon emissions/disclosures and firm value, Chava 2014 (`doi:10.1287/mnsc.2013.1863`) on environmental externalities and cost of capital, and El Ghoul et al. 2011 (`doi:10.1016/j.jbankfin.2011.02.007`) on CSR and cost of capital. Emerging-market-specific evidence appears thinner and less causally identified.  

Rationale: I read the most directly relevant foundations to extract mechanisms, empirical designs, and limitations that can be adapted to emerging markets and climate-specific disclosure regimes.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["doi:10.2308/accr.00000005","doi:10.2308/accr-50629","doi:10.1287/mnsc.2013.1863","doi:10.1016/j.jbankfin.2011.02.007"],"sections":["abstract","identification","results","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: Dhaliwal et al. find initiation of CSR reporting is associated with lower subsequent cost of equity, especially for firms with high prior cost of capital and stronger stakeholder orientation. Matsumura et al. show markets penalize carbon emissions but reward disclosure, while Chava finds environmental concerns raise both debt and equity costs. Most evidence is U.S./global-developed-market oriented and often relies on voluntary-disclosure selection rather than policy-driven variation.

full_prompt: Analyze the impact of high-frequency trading on market liquidity during periods of macroeconomic uncertainty. Return the output as gene_genome.
chosen:
Rationale: I first locate finance-market microstructure evidence linking HFT/algorithmic trading to liquidity, especially around macro news or high-uncertainty regimes.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"high-frequency trading liquidity macroeconomic uncertainty macro news announcements market quality"}
```  
[result]: Found core candidates: Hendershott, Jones & Menkveld (2011, DOI: 10.1111/j.1540-6261.2011.01624.x), Brogaard, Hendershott & Riordan (2014, DOI: 10.1093/rfs/hhu032), Menkveld (2013, DOI: 10.1016/j.jfineco.2013.02.009), and Scholtus, van Dijk & Frijns (2014, DOI: 10.1016/j.jbankfin.2014.09.016). The last paper is especially relevant because it studies algorithmic trading and market quality around macroeconomic news announcements.

Rationale: I read the most relevant empirical papers to separate “normal-times liquidity provision” from “uncertainty/news-event liquidity fragility.”  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1111/j.1540-6261.2011.01624.x","10.1093/rfs/hhu032","10.1016/j.jfineco.2013.02.009","10.1016/j.jbankfin.2014.09.016"]}
```  
[result]: Hendershott et al. find algorithmic trading improves quoted and effective spreads in normal conditions. Brogaard et al. show HFTs contribute to price discovery but can demand liquidity during volatile information events. Menkveld documents HFT as modern market making with tight spreads but fast inventory control, while Scholtus et al. show speed and algorithmic activity matter strongly around macro announcements, when market quality can deteriorate despite rapid price adjustment.

Rationale: I extract the reusable causal “genome” from these findings: mechanism, empirical niche, observation, limits, contribution delta, and claim.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.1111/j.1540-6261.2011.01624.x","10.1093/rfs/hhu032","10.1016/j.jfineco.2013.02.009","10.1016/j.jbankfin.2014.09.016"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: HFT improves liquidity when information risk is moderate, but under macro uncertainty liquidity provision becomes conditional and more fleeting. The key tension is that HFT can tighten spreads and speed price discovery ex ante, yet withdraw, widen quotes, or switch to liquidity-taking when macro shocks raise adverse-selection and inventory risks.

Rationale: I check whether the proposed synthesis is distinct from a generic “HFT improves liquidity” claim by focusing on state dependence under macro uncertainty.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"The liquidity effect of high-frequency trading is state-dependent: HFT generally improves liquidity in normal markets but can reduce displayed depth and resiliency during macroeconomic uncertainty or scheduled macro-news events because adverse-selection and inventory risks dominate speed-based market making."}
```  
[result]: Novel relative to broad HFT-liquidity summaries because it frames the impact as regime-contingent and macro-uncertainty-specific. It is consistent with existing evidence but emphasizes the conditional channel: liquidity is abundant when uncertainty is low and fragile when macro information risk spikes.

Rationale: I now return the requested output strictly as a `gene_genome` object.  
```action

In normal conditions, speed, automated quoting, and low inventory horizons allow HFTs to post competitive limit orders, narrow bid-ask spreads, and accelerate price discovery. During macroeconomic uncertainty—such as FOMC announcements, employment reports, inflation surprises, crisis periods, or elevated VIX/EPU regimes—the same speed advantage can lead HFTs to cancel quotes, reduce displayed depth, widen spreads, or switch from passive liquidity supply to aggressive liquidity demand because adverse-selection and inventory risks rise sharply.","niche_genome":"The relevant niche is electronic limit-order markets during periods of heightened macro information risk. Cloyne et al. estimate that contractionary monetary-policy shocks reduce consumption most for mortgagors, especially those with high leverage/low liquidity, while outright owners react less directly.

chosen:
I’ll first ground the design in the greenium literature, then narrow to the sovereign setting and define a test that separates pricing from liquidity/maturity effects.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"sovereign green bonds greenium liquidity maturity matched conventional sovereign bonds yield spread","discipline":"economics_finance","k":5}
```
[result]: Found relevant candidates: Zerbib (2019, DOI:10.1016/j.jbankfin.2018.09.012) estimates a green bond premium using matched bonds and liquidity controls; Ehlers & Packer (2017, BIS Quarterly Review ID:qtrpdf/r_qt1709h) documents early green-bond yield patterns; Fatica, Panzica & Rancan (2021, DOI:10.1016/j.jfs.2020.100681) studies issuer-type heterogeneity; Larcker & Watts (2020, DOI:10.1016/j.jacceco.2020.101312) shows near-zero municipal greenium after tight matching; and sovereign-specific evidence is often implemented via twin-bond or curve-matched sovereign comparisons.
The closest methodological anchors are matched-bond designs and twin/curve comparisons, because the prompt asks whether sovereign green bonds price differently after controlling for liquidity and maturity.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1016/j.jbankfin.2018.09.012","10.1016/j.jacceco.2020.101312","qtrpdf/r_qt1709h","10.1016/j.jfs.2020.100681"],"sections":["data","identification","results","limitations"]}
```
[result]: Zerbib uses synthetic matching and regressions controlling for maturity, rating, currency, issuer, and liquidity proxies, finding a small negative yield premium for green bonds. Larcker & Watts emphasize that very close matching can shrink estimated premia toward zero, highlighting liquidity and tax/maturity confounding. Ehlers & Packer and Fatica et al. support testing greenium as a yield-spread residual after accounting for bond characteristics and market depth.
I’ll extract the reusable research genome from these studies, focusing on what transfers to sovereign bonds.
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.1016/j.jbankfin.2018.09.012","10.1016/j.jacceco.2020.101312","10.1016/j.jfs.2020.100681"],"schema":"gene_genome"}
```
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = investor environmental preference or ESG mandate lowers required yield; niche = bonds with comparable issuer/currency/rating but varying green label; observation = yield or spread differentials estimated with matching/regressions; limitation = residual liquidity, maturity-curve mismatch, and selection into green issuance; delta = apply stronger sovereign within-issuer identification; claim = greenium exists only if the green label predicts lower yields after controls.

full_prompt: Study how exchange rate volatility affects export decisions among small and medium-sized firms. Return the output as gene_genome.
chosen:
Rationale: I first search for firm-level and SME-relevant evidence on exchange-rate volatility, export participation/intensity, and financial constraints, prioritizing papers with microdata and credible identification.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"exchange rate volatility export decisions small medium firms firm-level financial constraints"}
```  
[result]: Found candidates including `doi:10.1093/wber/lht035` Héricourt & Poncet (2015) on Chinese firms, `doi:10.1016/j.jimonfin.2013.08.026` Cheung & Sengupta (2013) on Indian firms, and `doi:10.1257/aer.104.7.1942` Amiti, Itskhoki & Konings (2014) on firm exchange-rate pass-through. The most directly relevant candidate links exchange-rate volatility to trade through financial constraints.

Rationale: I read the most relevant candidate plus complementary firm-level exchange-rate papers to anchor the mechanism and measurement choices.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["doi:10.1093/wber/lht035","doi:10.1016/j.jimonfin.2013.08.026","doi:10.1257/aer.104.7.1942"]}
```  
[result]: Héricourt & Poncet show exchange-rate volatility depresses firm exports and that the effect is stronger for financially constrained firms. Cheung & Sengupta find Indian firms’ export shares respond to exchange-rate movements with heterogeneity by firm characteristics, while Amiti et al. show large importers/exporters have different exchange-rate pass-through and exposure management than smaller firms.

Rationale: I extract the reusable “research genome” from the literature: sunk-cost export entry, volatility as uncertainty, and SME constraints as the key friction.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["doi:10.1093/wber/lht035","doi:10.1016/j.jimonfin.2013.08.026"],"target_schema":"gene_genome"}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanism: exchange-rate volatility raises profit uncertainty, discouraging export entry/continuation when firms face sunk market-entry costs and limited hedging. Extracted limitation: prior work often studies all firms or manufacturing exporters broadly, leaving SME-specific export initiation, destination choice, and hedging behavior less separately identified.

Rationale: I check novelty against the closest literature by focusing on SMEs’ extensive-margin export decisions rather than only aggregate export values or incumbent exporters’ sales.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","candidate_claim":"Exchange-rate volatility reduces SME export participation and destination expansion more strongly than for large firms because SMEs face higher fixed export costs, weaker access to currency hedging, and tighter external finance constraints.","against_paper_ids":["doi:10.1093/wber/lht035","doi:10.1016/j.jimonfin.2013.08.026","doi:10.1257/aer.104.7.1942"]}
```  
[result]: Novel but adjacent: the finance-constraint channel is established, but an SME-centered design emphasizing export entry/exit, destination diversification, and hedging access would add specificity. The strongest differentiator is separating intensive-margin export volumes from extensive-margin decisions among small and medium firms.

Rationale: I now synthesize a concise gene_genome suitable for an economics/finance study design.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","content":{"mechanism_genome":"Exchange-rate volatility increases uncertainty over foreign-currency revenues and local-currency profits. For SMEs, this uncertainty is amplified by sunk export-market entry costs, limited access to natural hedges/imported-input offsets, weaker use of financial hedging instruments, and tighter working-capital constraints. The expected result is lower probability of export entry, higher probability of export exit, fewer destination additions, and possibly reduced export intensity among continuing exporters.","niche_genome":"Small and medium-sized firms in tradable sectors, especially manufacturing and tradable services, observed in firm-level panel data linked to customs/export transactions, balance-sheet variables, and destination-specific exchange-rate volatility. The most relevant empirical setting is SMEs that are near the export threshold or intermittently export, because their decisions reveal extensive-margin sensitivity to uncertainty.","observation_genome":"Key outcomes: export participation, export entry, export exit, number of destinations, number of products exported, export value share, and use of currency hedging where available. Key treatment: bilateral or trade-weighted real exchange-rate volatility measured over rolling windows, interacted with firm size, financial constraints, foreign-currency debt, imported-input exposure, and destination portfolio concentration. Identification can exploit variation across destination currencies, industries with different export exposure, and time-varying exchange-rate volatility shocks.","limitation_genome":"Main threats are simultaneity between macro volatility and foreign demand, measurement error in SME hedging behavior, sample selection because informal or very small exporters may be missing, and difficulty separating volatility effects from exchange-rate level effects. Firm fixed effects, destination-year controls, industry-year controls, and controls for foreign demand are needed, but causal interpretation remains strongest when using plausibly exogenous currency-volatility shocks or predetermined destination exposure.","delta_genome":"Relative to broad firm-level exchange-rate studies, the contribution is to isolate SME export decisions at the extensive margin and test whether financial frictions and hedging constraints mediate the volatility effect. The study should distinguish export participation from export volume, and destination expansion from destination retention, because SMEs may respond to volatility mainly by avoiding entry into risky markets rather than by immediately cutting existing export sales.","claim_genome":"Exchange-rate volatility is expected to reduce SME export participation and destination expansion, with the largest negative effects among financially constrained, non-hedging, single-destination, and low-productivity SMEs. Larger firms or SMEs with imported-input natural hedges, foreign-currency revenues matched to costs, or access to derivative hedging should exhibit weaker responses. Therefore, exchange-rate volatility acts not only as a price-risk shock but as a barrier to internationalization for smaller firms."}}
```

full_prompt: Analyze whether corporate stock buybacks affect long-run productivity growth and investment intensity. Return the output as gene_genome.
chosen:
Rationale: Start by locating empirical corporate-finance evidence directly linking repurchases to real activity such as capital expenditure, employment, R&D, and productivity-relevant investment.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "stock repurchases buybacks real effects investment productivity long-run firm investment intensity",
  "discipline": "economics_finance",
  "top_k": 5
}
```

[result]: Candidate evidence includes Almeida, Fos & Kronlund, “The Real Effects of Share Repurchases,” *Journal of Financial Economics* 2016, paper_id `doi:10.1016/j.jfineco.2015.08.008`; Gutierrez & Philippon, “Investment-less Growth,” *Brookings Papers on Economic Activity* 2017, paper_id `doi:10.1353/eca.2017.0013`; and Kahle & Stulz, “Why Are Corporate Payouts So High in the 2000s?”, *Journal of Financial Economics* 2021, paper_id `doi:10.1016/j.jfineco.2020.10.001`.

Rationale: Read the core causal paper because it uses an identification strategy around EPS incentives and directly estimates whether repurchases crowd out real investment.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1016/j.jfineco.2015.08.008",
  "sections": ["abstract", "identification", "main results", "investment and employment results"]
}
```

[result]: Almeida, Fos & Kronlund find that firms just missing EPS thresholds increase repurchases, consistent with managerial incentives to boost EPS. These repurchases are associated with reductions in employment and capital expenditures, suggesting short-run financial payout pressure can crowd out real activity.

Rationale: Add macro/industry context to distinguish “buybacks mechanically reduce investment” from the broader explanation that low competition, intangible capital, and weak investment opportunities also shape payout-investment patterns.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1353/eca.2017.0013",
  "sections": ["abstract", "empirical patterns", "firm-level investment", "market power explanation"]
}
```

[result]: Gutierrez & Philippon document weak U.S. investment despite high profitability and valuations, attributing part of the gap to reduced competition and governance frictions. The evidence supports the view that high payouts and weak investment intensity co-occur, but buybacks are not always the primitive cause; market structure and investment opportunities mediate the relationship.

Rationale: Extract a structured causal genome from the literature, emphasizing mechanisms, observed empirical regularities, and limitations for long-run productivity inference.

```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "doi:10.1016/j.jfineco.2015.08.008",
    "doi:10.1353/eca.2017.0013",
    "doi:10.1016/j.jfineco.2020.10.001"
  ],
  "schema": "gene_genome"
}
```

[result]: The extracted genome identifies an EPS-management channel, payout-investment tradeoff, and governance/market-power moderators. It also flags a major limitation: firm-level buyback regressions often identify short- to medium-run capital expenditure and employment responses, while long-run productivity growth requires longer horizons and careful separation of causality from selection into mature, low-growth firms.

Rationale: Check whether the synthesized claim is novel and balanced relative to the literature: buybacks can depress investment intensity under incentive pressure, but the long-run productivity effect is conditional rather than universal.

```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "claim": "Corporate stock buybacks reduce long-run productivity growth primarily when driven by short-term EPS or payout incentives that crowd out capex, R&D, and organizational investment; however, aggregate productivity effects are heterogeneous because mature firms with weak investment opportunities may rationally return cash without sacrificing high-return projects.",
  "against_paper_ids": [
    "doi:10.1016/j.jfineco.2015.08.008",
    "doi:10.1353/eca.2017.0013",
    "doi:10.1016/j.jfineco.2020.10.001"
  ]
}
```

[result]: The claim is consistent with existing evidence but adds a useful conditional synthesis: the productivity effect depends on the motive for repurchases and the availability of positive-NPV investment. The strongest supported causal link is from EPS-motivated buybacks to lower investment intensity, while evidence on economy-wide long-run productivity remains more indirect.

Rationale: Propose the final six-field `gene_genome` requested by the prompt.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "content": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Corporate stock buybacks can affect long-run productivity through a capital-allocation channel. When managers repurchase shares to meet EPS targets, support stock prices, or satisfy short-horizon investors, internal funds are redirected from capital expenditure, R&D, worker training, and intangible capability building toward payouts. This can lower investment intensity and slow productivity growth if the forgone projects had positive expected productivity returns. However, when firms are mature, cash-rich, and lack high-return investment opportunities, repurchases may be an efficient distribution mechanism rather than a cause of underinvestment.",
    "niche_genome": "Most relevant for publicly listed firms with strong equity-based managerial incentives, analyst EPS pressure, activist or short-horizon ownership, high free cash flow, and discretionary investment margins. The channel is especially plausible in industries where productivity growth depends on sustained intangible investment, R&D, employee capabilities, or long-cycle capital projects. It is weaker for firms with poor investment opportunities, abundant external financing access, or repurchases funded from genuinely excess cash.",
    "observation_genome": "Empirical evidence from Almeida, Fos & Kronlund 2016 shows that firms near EPS thresholds increase repurchases and subsequently reduce real activity such as employment and capital expenditure, supporting a causal payout-investment tradeoff. Gutierrez & Philippon 2017 document weak investment despite high profits and valuations, consistent with high payouts accompanying an investment shortfall, though they emphasize competition and governance as underlying causes. Kahle & Stulz 2021 caution that high payouts also reflect profitability, firm maturity, and changing asset composition, so aggregate buybacks do not mechanically imply forgone productive investment.",
    "limitation_genome": "The strongest causal evidence concerns short- to medium-run investment intensity, not direct long-run total factor productivity. Buyback firms are often mature and may have fewer productive projects, creating selection bias. Accounting data under-measure intangible investment, making it difficult to distinguish productivity-enhancing R&D and organizational spending from operating expenses. Aggregate productivity effects are also mediated by whether cash returned through buybacks is reallocated by investors to younger, higher-growth firms.",
    "delta_genome": "A credible empirical design should separate incentive-driven repurchases from lifecycle-driven repurchases. The key delta is to estimate whether exogenous or quasi-exogenous increases in buyback pressure reduce future capex, R&D, patenting, labor productivity, and TFP relative to matched firms with similar investment opportunities. Useful variation includes EPS-threshold pressure, tax or regulatory shocks to repurchases, activist interventions, executive compensation vesting schedules, or blackout-window constraints.",
    "claim_genome": "Corporate stock buybacks can depress long-run productivity growth and investment intensity, but the effect is conditional rather than universal. The evidence is strongest that short-term, EPS-motivated repurchases crowd out investment and employment, creating a plausible drag on future productivity. In contrast, buybacks by mature firms with limited positive-NPV projects may have little negative productivity effect and may improve capital allocation if funds flow to more productive firms. Therefore, the central hypothesis should be framed as: buybacks harm long-run productivity when they are driven by short-term financial incentives and occur in firms with valuable but deferrable investment opportunities."
  }
}
```

[result]: gene_genome proposed.

full_prompt: Examine the macroeconomic effects of universal basic income pilots on labor supply, consumption, and local prices. Return the output as gene_genome.
chosen:
Rationale: I first search for credible quasi-experimental or randomized evidence that covers the three requested macro-relevant margins: labor supply, consumption/demand, and local prices/general equilibrium.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"universal basic income pilots labor supply consumption local prices general equilibrium evidence economics","discipline":"economics_finance","limit":5}
```  
[result]: Candidate evidence clusters found: Kenya large-scale cash/UBI-style transfers with village-market general equilibrium (`doi:10.3982/ECTA17945`); Alaska Permanent Fund universal cash dividends and labor supply (`nber:w24312`); Finland basic income experiment employment and welfare (`kela:working-papers-2020-9`). The Kenya study is strongest on consumption and prices; Alaska and Finland are strongest on labor supply.

Rationale: I read the core papers to extract mechanisms and identify whether price effects are local-equilibrium responses rather than purely household-level outcomes.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["doi:10.3982/ECTA17945","nber:w24312","kela:working-papers-2020-9"],"focus":["labor supply","consumption","local prices","general equilibrium","cash transfer design"]}
```  
[result]: Egger et al. report large expenditure and income multipliers with little inflation in treated Kenyan markets. Jones & Marinescu find Alaska dividends do not reduce aggregate employment, with some shift toward part-time work. Finland’s pilot shows small/limited employment effects but improved well-being, with weaker macro inference because treatment was individual-level and not designed to move local prices.

Rationale: I extract a compact gene genome from the evidence, separating mechanism, niche, observations, limitations, empirical delta, and claim.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["doi:10.3982/ECTA17945","nber:w24312","kela:working-papers-2020-9"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: The extracted genome emphasizes liquidity/wealth effects, demand stimulus, and local production response. Main limitations are external validity from pilots, finite transfer duration, financing not modeled in most pilots, and weak evidence on national-scale price dynamics.

Rationale: I check whether the synthesized claim is already covered by a single paper or instead requires cross-study synthesis across different pilot designs and equilibrium margins.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"UBI pilots tend to raise consumption without large labor-supply reductions or local inflation when transfers are credible and markets have slack, but macro effects depend on financing, scale, and supply elasticity.","against":["doi:10.3982/ECTA17945","nber:w24312","kela:working-papers-2020-9"]}
```  
[result]: The claim is a synthesis rather than a duplicate of one study: Egger et al. covers GE consumption/prices, Alaska covers permanent universal dividend labor effects, and Finland covers individual-level labor response. The financing and national-scale inflation caveats remain under-identified.

Rationale: I now return the requested `gene_genome` output, grounded in the papers and scoped to macroeconomic effects of UBI-style pilots.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","output_type":"gene_genome","content":{"mechanism_genome":"Universal basic income pilots operate through an unconditional income shock: households receive liquidity/wealth that relaxes borrowing constraints, raises consumption demand, and may reduce labor supply through an income effect. In local economies with idle capacity or elastic supply, added demand can increase output, profits, and earnings rather than prices; where supply is inelastic, the same demand impulse can bid up local prices. Labor effects depend on transfer size, permanence, individual versus household targeting, and whether people use the transfer to search longer, reduce hours, start enterprises, or shift into part-time/nonmarket activity.","niche_genome":"Best-evidenced settings are cash-transfer or UBI-adjacent pilots rather than fully financed national UBI systems: rural Kenya’s large-scale village cash transfers with measured general-equilibrium spillovers (`doi:10.3982/ECTA17945`), Alaska’s universal annual Permanent Fund dividend (`nber:w24312`), and Finland’s randomized basic-income experiment among unemployed individuals (`kela:working-papers-2020-9`). These settings are informative for local demand, labor-market participation, and price pass-through, but less definitive for national tax-financed UBI.","observation_genome":"Across the strongest evidence, unconditional transfers generally raise household consumption and subjective welfare. Labor-supply reductions are small or statistically limited: Alaska shows no meaningful fall in aggregate employment, though part-time work rises; Finland shows limited employment gains or losses but improved well-being. In Kenya, transfers generate sizable local expenditure and income increases with positive spillovers to nonrecipients and little measured local inflation, implying that local supply responses absorbed much of the demand shock.","limitation_genome":"Pilot evidence is not the same as a permanent national UBI. Most pilots are externally funded, so they omit the distortionary or distributional effects of taxes needed to finance a real program.
