full_prompt: Investigate how remittance shocks affect household investment in education and entrepreneurship. Return the output as gene_genome.
chosen:
Rationale: Start from canonical empirical work linking exogenous remittance variation to household investments in human capital and enterprise formation.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "remittance shocks household education entrepreneurship investment exchange rate shocks Yang 2008 Philippines Woodruff Zenteno Mexico",
  "discipline": "economics_finance",
  "top_k": 5
}
```  
[result]: Found core candidates: Yang (2008) on Philippine migrant exchange-rate shocks and household investment; Edwards & Ureta (2003) on remittances and schooling in El Salvador; Woodruff & Zenteno (2007) on migration networks and microenterprises in Mexico.

Rationale: Read the most relevant studies with credible identification strategies and outcomes covering education and entrepreneurship.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_ids": [
    "10.1111/j.1468-0297.2008.02134.x",
    "10.1016/S0304-3878(03)00115-9",
    "10.1016/j.jdeveco.2006.03.006"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: Yang (2008) uses exchange-rate shocks during the Asian financial crisis as plausibly exogenous variation in remittance purchasing power. Edwards & Ureta (2003) estimate remittance associations with school retention. Woodruff & Zenteno (2007) link migration networks/remittance access to microenterprise investment in Mexico.

Rationale: Extract the research “genome” from prior work to identify mechanisms, empirical niches, observations, and limitations.  
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "10.1111/j.1468-0297.2008.02134.x",
    "10.1016/S0304-3878(03)00115-9",
    "10.1016/j.jdeveco.2006.03.006"
  ],
  "schema": [
    "mechanism_genome",
    "niche_genome",
    "observation_genome",
    "limitation_genome",
    "delta_genome",
    "claim_genome"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: The literature supports liquidity-relaxation and risk-insurance channels: positive remittance shocks increase schooling expenditures and may finance business capital. Main limits are potential migration selection, conflation of remittance income with migrant absence, and weaker evidence on joint education–entrepreneurship allocation within the same household.

Rationale: Check whether a sharper contribution can be made by studying portfolio reallocation across education and entrepreneurship after remittance shocks, rather than each outcome separately.  
```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "candidate_claim": "Exogenous remittance shocks alter household investment portfolios by shifting resources jointly across education and entrepreneurship, with heterogeneous effects by credit constraints, child age, and local business opportunities.",
  "against_paper_ids": [
    "10.1111/j.1468-0297.2008.02134.x",
    "10.1016/S0304-3878(03)00115-9",
    "10.1016/j.jdeveco.2006.03.006"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: The claim is related to Yang (2008), but the joint portfolio-allocation framing is relatively less developed. Prior papers often emphasize either schooling or enterprise investment, leaving scope to model complementarities and tradeoffs between human-capital and business-capital responses.

Rationale: Propose the final gene_genome synthesis, grounded in the evidence and highlighting a tractable empirical contribution.  
```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "output_schema": "gene_genome",
  "gene_genome": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Remittance shocks affect household investment through three linked channels: first, a liquidity channel, where higher remittance purchasing power relaxes borrowing constraints and allows households to pay school fees, buy supplies, or finance microenterprise capital; second, an insurance channel, where remittances reduce income risk and make longer-horizon investments such as secondary schooling or business entry more attractive; third, a labor-substitution channel, where migrant absence can raise household labor needs and potentially offset schooling gains or push members into self-employment.",
    "niche_genome": "The strongest empirical niche is households with migrants whose remittance value changes for reasons plausibly exogenous to household demand, such as exchange-rate movements in destination countries. Canonical evidence comes from Philippine households during the Asian financial crisis, Salvadoran households receiving international remittances, and Mexican communities with migration networks that affect access to migrant finance for microenterprise activity.",
    "observation_genome": "Existing evidence indicates that positive remittance shocks tend to increase education-related investment, including school attendance, retention, and education spending, especially where credit constraints are binding. Evidence also suggests that migration-linked resources can increase entrepreneurship or microenterprise investment by financing start-up capital and working capital. However, the response is heterogeneous: younger children may experience larger schooling effects, while households with adult labor and local market opportunities may be more likely to channel remittances into enterprise formation.",
    "limitation_genome": "Key limitations are selection into migration, imperfect separation of remittance-income effects from migrant-absence effects, and limited observation of intra-household allocation rules. Many studies examine schooling or entrepreneurship separately, making it difficult to know whether remittance shocks create complementarities between education and enterprise investment or induce tradeoffs. Measurement of informal business assets and irregular remittance flows is also noisy.",
    "delta_genome": "A useful next step is to estimate how exogenous remittance shocks change the household investment portfolio across education and entrepreneurship jointly. The empirical design should exploit destination-country exchange-rate shocks interacted with pre-existing migrant location, then estimate effects on schooling expenditures, enrollment, business entry, business assets, and labor allocation within the same household. Heterogeneity by baseline credit constraints, child age, gender, and local enterprise returns would distinguish liquidity effects from labor-substitution and risk-insurance mechanisms.",
    "claim_genome": "Positive remittance shocks are likely to raise household investment, but their allocation depends on household constraints and local returns: credit-constrained households with school-age children mainly increase education investment, while households with available adult labor and profitable local opportunities are more likely to expand entrepreneurship. The central research claim is that remittance shocks should be modeled not simply as income changes, but as shocks to the household’s intertemporal investment portfolio across human capital and business capital."
  }
}
```

full_prompt: Study whether ESG fund flows create measurable asset pricing distortions in equity markets. Return the output as gene_genome.

[result]: Found core candidates: Hartzmark & Sussman (2019, JF, paper_id: `10.1111/jofi.12841`) on Morningstar sustainability ratings causing fund flows; Pástor, Stambaugh & Taylor (2021, JFE, paper_id: `10.1016/j.jfineco.2020.12.011`) on sustainable investing equilibrium; Pástor, Stambaugh & Taylor (2022, JFE, paper_id: `10.1016/j.jfineco.2022.07.007`) on green stock outperformance from climate-concern shocks; Pedersen, Fitzgibbons & Pomorski (2021, JFE, paper_id: `10.1016/j.jfineco.2020.11.001`) on ESG-efficient frontier.  

Rationale: Read the most directly relevant empirical papers: one identifies ESG-induced mutual-fund flow shocks, and one links green-minus-brown returns to demand/preference shocks.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1111/jofi.12841","10.1016/j.jfineco.2022.07.007"]}
```  
[result]: Hartzmark & Sussman show high-sustainability Morningstar globe ratings attract net inflows while low ratings experience outflows, using a rating introduction shock. Pástor et al. show green stocks outperformed brown stocks during periods of rising climate concern, consistent with realized returns reflecting unexpected demand shifts rather than permanently higher expected returns.  

Rationale: Extract reusable research genes from the literature and identify what remains under-tested for asset-pricing distortion caused specifically by ESG fund flows.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.1111/jofi.12841","10.1016/j.jfineco.2020.12.011","10.1016/j.jfineco.2022.07.007"],"schema":"gene_genome"}
```  
[result]: Existing mechanisms emphasize investor preference shocks, constrained capital, and price pressure; existing observations document ESG-label-induced fund flows and green-brown return spreads. The key limitation is that fund-level ESG flow shocks are rarely mapped through portfolio holdings into stock-level abnormal demand, temporary price pressure, return reversals, and valuation distortions.  

Rationale: Check whether the proposed contribution is novel relative to the closest literature: identifying stock-level pricing distortions caused by ESG fund flows rather than merely documenting ESG preferences or green returns.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"Use plausibly exogenous ESG mutual fund flow shocks, mapped through lagged holdings, to test whether ESG demand creates stock-level price pressure, valuation premia, and subsequent return reversals in equities.","paper_ids":["10.1111/jofi.12841","10.1016/j.jfineco.2022.07.007","10.1016/j.jfineco.2020.12.011","10.1016/j.jfineco.2020.11.001"]}
```  
[result]: Novelty is moderate-to-high: prior work establishes ESG preferences, equilibrium implications, and aggregate green returns, but a holdings-implied flow instrument can isolate stock-level ESG-demand pressure and distinguish persistent repricing from transitory distortion via reversal tests.  

Rationale: Propose the research design as a gene_genome, emphasizing mechanism, empirical niche, observable tests, limitations, marginal contribution, and central claim.

Compare effects for ESG versus non-ESG fund flows and for green/high-ESG versus brown/low-ESG firms.","limitation_genome":"Identification is threatened if ESG fund flows coincide with omitted information about firms’ future cash flows, regulation, or climate risk. full_prompt: Examine how geopolitical risk announcements affect commodity futures prices and hedging demand. Return the output as gene_genome.
chosen:
Rationale: Start by anchoring the question in two literatures: measured geopolitical-risk news shocks and commodity futures/hedging pressure.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "geopolitical risk announcements commodity futures prices hedging demand CFTC hedging pressure futures returns",
  "discipline": "economics_finance",
  "top_k": 5
}
```  
[result]: Candidate foundations include Caldara & Iacoviello’s geopolitical risk index paper (`doi:10.1257/aer.20191823`), Acharya-Lochstoer-Ramadorai on commodity hedging pressure and limits to arbitrage (`doi:10.1016/j.jfineco.2013.03.003`), and Bessembinder’s hedging-pressure risk-premium evidence (`doi:10.1093/rfs/5.4.637`). Existing work links GPR to commodity returns/volatility, but less directly to intraday announcement surprises and subsequent commercial hedging demand.

Rationale: Read the most relevant papers to extract mechanisms, measurement choices, and gaps.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_ids": [
    "doi:10.1257/aer.20191823",
    "doi:10.1016/j.jfineco.2013.03.003",
    "doi:10.1093/rfs/5.4.637"
  ],
  "focus": [
    "geopolitical risk measurement and event timing",
    "commodity futures price response",
    "hedging pressure / commercial trader demand measurement"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: Caldara & Iacoviello provide a news-based GPR index and distinguish threat versus act components. Acharya et al. and Bessembinder show that hedging pressure and intermediary constraints help price commodity futures risk premia, commonly using CFTC trader positions and futures basis/returns.

Rationale: Convert the literature into a structured genome so the proposed study can preserve known mechanisms while targeting a sharper empirical gap.  
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "doi:10.1257/aer.20191823",
    "doi:10.1016/j.jfineco.2013.03.003",
    "doi:10.1093/rfs/5.4.637"
  ],
  "schema": "gene_genome"
}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: GPR news raises uncertainty and precautionary inventory/portfolio demand; commodity futures embed both physical-supply risk and financial risk premia; hedging demand is observable in commercial net positions but usually at weekly frequency. Key limitation: announcement-level identification of GPR shocks is rarely connected to dynamic hedger-position adjustment.

[result]: Found core candidates: Gruber (1997, AER, DOI: `10.1257/aer.87.1.192`), Ganong & Noel (2019, AER, DOI: `10.1257/aer.20170537`), Kroft & Notowidigdo (2016, ReStud, DOI: `10.1093/restud/rdw009`), and Hsu, Matsa & Melzer (2018, AER, DOI: `10.1257/aer.20140989`). These cover direct consumption smoothing, spending dynamics at benefit exhaustion, state-dependent UI, and stabilization spillovers.

Rationale: Read the most directly relevant evidence on whether more generous UI stabilizes household spending, especially in downturns.  
```action

  "tool": "read",

    "consumption smoothing",
    "recession or high-unemployment states",
    "household liquidity constraints",
    "macroeconomic stabilization"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: Gruber estimates that higher replacement rates substantially reduce the fall in food consumption upon unemployment. Ganong & Noel show spending drops sharply at UI exhaustion, implying benefits stabilize consumption while paid. Kroft & Notowidigdo find the efficiency cost of UI is lower in high-unemployment periods, supporting more generous recession UI. Hsu et al. show UI mitigates mortgage delinquency and local housing distress.

Rationale: Extract a structured research genome from the literature, separating mechanism, empirical setting, and limitations.  
```action

  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "10.1257/aer.87.1.192",

    "10.1093/restud/rdw009",
    "10.1257/aer.20140989"
  ],
  "schema": "gene_genome"
}
```  
[result]: The literature consistently identifies liquidity replacement as the key mechanism: UI transfers sustain spending when earnings collapse. Evidence is strongest for constrained households and for spending categories tied to nondurable consumption, debt service, and housing stability, but less complete on aggregate recession multipliers and heterogeneity across benefit extensions versus weekly benefit levels.

Rationale: Check whether the proposed claim is novel relative to existing work or should be framed as a synthesis/evaluation.  
```action

  "tool": "novelty_check",

  "against_paper_ids": [

    "10.1257/aer.20170537",
    "10.1093/restud/rdw009",
    "10.1257/aer.20140989"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: The broad claim is not novel as a standalone contribution; it is well supported by existing evidence. A more defensible contribution is an evidence-grounded evaluation emphasizing recession-state dependence: UI stabilizes consumption most when unemployment risk is high, liquidity constraints bind, and benefit exhaustion would otherwise trigger spending declines.

Rationale: Return the requested gene_genome evaluating whether UI generosity stabilizes consumption during recessions.  
```action

During recessions, this mechanism is amplified because unemployment spells are longer, reemployment probabilities are lower, and private risk-sharing becomes less effective.",
    "niche_genome": "The relevant setting is recessionary or high-unemployment labor markets in which job losers face persistent income losses and constrained access to credit.     "observation_genome": "Empirical evidence supports a consumption-smoothing role for UI. Kroft and Notowidigdo (2016, DOI: 10.1093/restud/rdw009) provide evidence that the behavioral cost of UI is lower in high-unemployment states, strengthening the case for recession-contingent generosity. (2016, JPE, DOI:10.1086/684581) on carbon taxes and directed technical change; Calel & Dechezleprêtre (2016, RESTAT, DOI:10.1162/REST_a_00470) on carbon pricing and low-carbon innovation under the EU ETS.
```

Rationale: Read the most directly relevant firm-level carbon-tax paper and complementary evidence on employment and technology responses.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1016/j.jpubeco.2014.04.016","10.1016/j.jeem.2017.03.003","10.1086/684581","10.1162/REST_a_00470"],"focus":["firm profitability or competitiveness","employment","technology adoption or innovation","identification design","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: Martin et al. exploit UK CCL exemptions/discounts and find reduced energy intensity with little evidence of adverse effects on employment, gross output, or productivity. Yamazaki finds BC’s revenue-neutral carbon tax reallocates employment—losses in carbon-intensive sectors but gains in cleaner/service sectors. Aghion et al. show carbon-tax-inclusive fuel prices redirect auto innovation toward clean patents, while Calel & Dechezleprêtre show carbon pricing can raise low-carbon patenting without clear evidence of crowding out.

Rationale: Extract the reusable causal “genome” from the literature before identifying the gap for a focused evaluation of profitability, employment, and adoption.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.1016/j.jpubeco.2014.04.016","10.1016/j.jeem.2017.03.003","10.1086/684581"],"schema":"gene_genome"}
```  
[result]: Existing evidence emphasizes three mechanisms: carbon taxes raise effective energy/input costs; firms respond through energy-saving process changes, output-price pass-through, and clean innovation; labor effects depend on sectoral exposure and revenue recycling. Limitations are that profitability is often proxied indirectly, technology adoption is frequently measured by patents rather than plant-level adoption, and most studies examine one jurisdiction or outcome at a time.

Rationale: Check whether a combined profitability–employment–technology-adoption design would be incremental relative to the identified literature.

[result]: Novelty is moderate-to-high if profitability is measured directly using accounting margins/ROA, employment is tracked within firms rather than only sectors, and technological adoption is observed at the firm or plant level. The contribution is strongest when exploiting tax-rate changes, exemptions, thresholds, or fuel-mix exposure in a unified event-study/triple-difference framework.

The papers use rent-control shocks in San Francisco, Cambridge, and Massachusetts to estimate tenant mobility, landlord conversion, and housing-market effects.

Autor et al. exploit Cambridge rent-control removal and find large capitalization and spillover effects, but the design is less directly about new construction starts.

(2019, `NBER:w25416`) on reversal-rate mechanisms; Basten & Mariathasan (2018, `CESifo:2288`) on Swiss bank responses; and Altavilla et al. (2018, `ECB:WP2289`) on bank profitability under monetary accommodation.

Rationale: Read the most relevant causal papers to extract identification assumptions, treatment variation, outcomes, and gaps for a feasible research strategy.

[result]: The literature commonly exploits cross-bank exposure to deposit funding or excess reserves around NIRP adoption. Findings suggest compressed net interest margins for deposit-reliant banks, partial substitution into fees/riskier lending, and heterogeneous lending effects depending on capitalization, liquidity, and ability to pass rates through to depositors.

Rationale: Check whether a strategy combining bank profitability, borrower-level lending behavior, and heterogeneous pass-through is sufficiently distinct from prior work.

`mechanism_genome`: Corporate tax cuts may increase after-tax returns, investment, labor demand, and bargaining surplus, but may also flow to dividends and repurchases.  
`niche_genome`: Prior work often studies state tax variation, German municipal tax changes, dividend-tax reform, or narrow investment incentives.  
`observation_genome`: Wages and employment sometimes respond positively to corporate tax reductions, but effects are heterogeneous and not always linked to shareholder payouts.  
`limitation_genome`: Many studies examine either labor outcomes or financial payouts, not both in a unified firm-level design around the same shock.  
`delta_genome`: A firm-level TCJA-style exposure design can jointly estimate effects on wages, employment, investment, dividends, and repurchases.  
`claim_genome`: The key empirical question is whether marginal corporate tax windfalls primarily benefit workers through real activity or shareholders through payout policy.

Rationale: Check whether the proposed contribution is sufficiently distinct from existing corporate-tax incidence studies.


[result]: Found highly relevant candidates including Cornaggia, Cornaggia & Israelsen, “Credit Ratings and the Cost of Municipal Financing,” Review of Financial Studies, paper_id: DOI:10.1093/rfs/hhy084; Adelino, Cunha & Ferreira, “The Economic Effects of Public Financing,” Review of Financial Studies, paper_id: DOI:10.1093/rfs/hhv076; and Bergstresser, Cohen & Shenai, “Financial Guarantors and the 2007–2009 Credit Crisis,” paper_id: NBER:w17082.

[result]: Cornaggia, Cornaggia & Israelsen use Moody’s 2010 recalibration of municipal ratings as a quasi-exogenous ratings shock and show rating changes lower municipal yields, especially where ratings matter for investor constraints. The paper establishes that ratings can affect costs independent of issuer fundamentals, but the shock is primarily upward recalibration rather than deterioration/downgrade events.  

rationale: To avoid duplicating the recalibration design, I’ll check novelty for a downgrade-focused design using forced-selling and primary-market issuance outcomes.

[result]: Novel relative to the main municipal ratings recalibration paper because it focuses on negative rating shocks and new-issue borrowing costs rather than broad upward recalibration. Strongest contribution is to combine downgrade-event timing, rating-threshold discontinuities, investor-clientele mechanisms, and primary-market borrowing outcomes.

full_prompt: Propose an empirical plan for measuring the influence of social media sentiment on retail investor trading and volatility. Return the output as idea_plan.
chosen:
Rationale: I first search for foundational and recent empirical finance work linking online sentiment, retail trading proxies, and volatility so the plan can be benchmarked against established identification and measurement choices.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"social media sentiment retail investor trading volatility empirical finance StockTwits Twitter retail order imbalance TAQ"}
```  
[result]: Found core candidates: Antweiler & Frank (2004, DOI: 10.1111/j.1540-6261.2004.00662.x) on message-board talk and volatility; Chen et al. (2014, DOI: 10.1093/rfs/hhu001) on social-media stock opinions and returns; Boehmer et al. (2021, DOI: 10.1111/jofi.13033) on TAQ-based retail-trade identification; and Cookson & Niessner (2020, DOI: 10.1093/rfs/hhz070) on belief disagreement on StockTwits.

Rationale: I read the most relevant papers to extract what is already known and where an empirical plan can add value: sentiment construction, retail-trade measurement, and volatility outcomes.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1111/j.1540-6261.2004.00662.x","10.1093/rfs/hhu001","10.1111/jofi.13033","10.1093/rfs/hhz070"]}
```  
[result]: Prior work shows that online message volume and bullishness predict trading activity and some return/volatility outcomes, but many studies rely on aggregate boards or returns rather than directly measured retail order imbalance. Boehmer et al. provide a feasible stock-day retail trading measure from TAQ, while StockTwits-style data allow stock-specific sentiment and disagreement.

Rationale: I check whether the proposed design is incremental rather than merely replicating prior sentiment-return studies.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","paper_ids":["10.1111/j.1540-6261.2004.00662.x","10.1093/rfs/hhu001","10.1111/jofi.13033","10.1093/rfs/hhz070"],"proposed_contribution":"Measure whether stock-level social media sentiment affects retail investor trading and volatility by combining StockTwits/Reddit sentiment with TAQ-derived retail order imbalance, intraday realized volatility, and a panel event-study/IV design around sentiment shocks and platform outages."}
```  
[result]: The contribution is plausible if framed as a trading-channel test: social media sentiment → retail order imbalance → realized volatility. The clearest novelty is combining high-frequency stock-specific sentiment with direct retail-trade classification and decomposing volatility effects into direct sentiment effects versus retail-flow-mediated effects.

Rationale: I now translate the evidence into a concrete empirical plan with datasets, implementation sequence, and evaluation metrics.

full_prompt: Build an idea plan for analyzing whether inflation-indexed wages amplify or dampen inflation persistence. Return the output as idea_plan.

```action

```
[result]: Found core candidates: Gray (1976) `doi:10.1016/0304-3932(76)90039-0`, Fischer (1977) `doi:10.1016/0167-2231(77)90004-7`, Smets & Wouters (2007) `doi:10.1257/aer.97.3.586`, Cogley & Sbordone (2008) `doi:10.1257/aer.98.5.2101`, and micro/institutional work on cost-of-living adjustment clauses in wage contracts.

Rationale: Read the most relevant theory and persistence papers to distinguish the expected amplification channel from possible dampening channels.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["doi:10.1016/0304-3932(76)90039-0","doi:10.1016/0167-2231(77)90004-7","doi:10.1257/aer.97.3.586","doi:10.1257/aer.98.5.2101"],"sections":["model","identification","results","conclusion"]}
```
[result]: Gray and Fischer show wage indexation can stabilize real wages and output under some shocks but can transmit price shocks into nominal wages. Smets-Wouters uses wage/price indexation as a friction generating persistence, while Cogley-Sbordone argues apparent indexation/persistence can be confounded with trend inflation and expectations.

Rationale: Extract a structured genome from the literature to identify what the new study should change relative to existing approaches.
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["doi:10.1257/aer.97.3.586","doi:10.1257/aer.98.5.2101"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```
[result]: `mechanism_genome`: wage/price indexation mechanically links past inflation to current wage-setting and marginal costs; `niche_genome`: aggregate NK/DSGE estimation; `observation_genome`: inflation persistence is partly attributed to backward-looking indexation; `limitation_genome`: weak direct measurement of actual indexed wage exposure; `delta_genome`: use micro/institutional variation in wage indexation; `claim_genome`: distinguish causal indexation effects from trend-inflation and expectations confounds.

Rationale: Check novelty of a causal empirical design using heterogeneity in indexed wage contracts rather than only calibrating/estimating indexation inside aggregate models.
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Estimate whether exposure to inflation-indexed wage contracts amplifies or dampens inflation persistence using sector/region-level variation in wage indexation clauses, local projections, and shock interactions.","against_paper_ids":["doi:10.1016/0304-3932(76)90039-0","doi:10.1016/0167-2231(77)90004-7","doi:10.1257/aer.97.3.586","doi:10.1257/aer.98.5.2101"],"criteria":["mechanism_distinctness","identification","data_novelty","policy_relevance"]}
```
[result]: Novelty is moderate-to-high: existing work establishes theoretical and aggregate-model channels, but less often directly estimates causal persistence effects from observed wage-indexation exposure. The strongest contribution is combining contract-level or sectoral indexation data with identified inflation shocks and impulse-response comparisons.

full_prompt: Investigate whether precision spectroscopy of antihydrogen can reveal CPT-violating effects. Provide gene_genome output identifying transition choices, uncertainty budgets, and theory targets.
chosen:
Rationale: Start from experimental antihydrogen spectroscopy papers and SME theory papers so the genome is anchored in measured transitions, quoted precision, and CPT-sensitive theory coefficients.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "antihydrogen precision spectroscopy CPT violation 1S-2S hyperfine ALPHA SME Kostelecky",
  "discipline": "physics",
  "max_results": 6
}
```  
[result]: Found core candidates: ALPHA observation of 1S–2S antihydrogen spectroscopy (`arXiv:1612.04723`), improved 1S–2S characterization (`arXiv:1804.03352`), ground-state hyperfine spectroscopy (`arXiv:1704.02389`), antihydrogen fine structure/Lamb shift (`arXiv:2001.02227`), and SME theory targets for H/\={H} CPT tests (`arXiv:hep-ph/9810269`, `arXiv:1506.01706`).  

Rationale: Read the highest-value experimental papers to identify transition choices and uncertainty budgets: 1S–2S gives ultra-high fractional precision, microwave hyperfine probes spin-dependent CPT/Lorentz terms, and 2S–2P/Lamb-shift-like measurements probe different bound-state combinations.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_ids": [
    "arXiv:1612.04723",
    "arXiv:1804.03352",
    "arXiv:1704.02389",
    "arXiv:2001.02227"
  ],
  "extract": [
    "transitions_measured",
    "reported_precision_or_limits",
    "dominant_uncertainties",
    "comparison_to_hydrogen"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: ALPHA measured trapped antihydrogen 1S–2S and found agreement with hydrogen at about the 10^-12 fractional level, with budgets dominated by trap-field/Zeeman effects, AC Stark shifts, statistics, and line-shape modeling. Hyperfine/fine-structure work targeted spin and 2P/2S-sensitive splittings, with larger absolute uncertainties but better sensitivity to some SME spin-dependent and magnetic-field-coupled CPT coefficients.  

Rationale: Extract a structured “gene genome” from experiment plus theory, separating mechanism, niche, observations, limitations, deltas, and claims.  
```action

  "paper_ids": [

    "arXiv:1506.01706"
  ],
  "schema": [
    "mechanism_genome",
    "niche_genome",
    "observation_genome",
    "limitation_genome",
    "delta_genome",
    "claim_genome"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: 1S–2S is the flagship CPT comparison but leading minimal-SME shifts can cancel or be suppressed depending on state/field configuration; hyperfine and Zeeman-resolved transitions directly probe spin-dependent proton/antiproton and electron/positron coefficients; fine-structure/Lamb-shift transitions add complementary sensitivity to bound-state QED and nonminimal SME terms.  

Rationale: Check whether the synthesis is novel relative to the literature: the question is not “has CPT been tested?” but whether a transition/uncertainty/theory-target map identifies the best near-term path.  
```action

  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "claim": "A combined antihydrogen spectroscopy program should prioritize field-controlled 1S-2S, Zeeman-resolved ground-state hyperfine, and 2S-2P/fine-structure transitions because their uncertainty budgets and SME coefficient sensitivities are complementary rather than redundant.",

    "arXiv:1804.03352",
    "arXiv:1704.02389",
    "arXiv:2001.02227",
    "arXiv:1506.01706"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: The component ideas are established, but the explicit genome tying transition choice to uncertainty budget and CPT-theory target is a useful synthesis. Novelty is integrative rather than proposing a fundamentally new transition.

full_prompt: Explore machine-learning-assisted reconstruction of particle collision events in high-luminosity experiments. Provide gene_genome output describing training data, physics constraints, and validation metrics.
chosen:
Rationale: I first locate representative HL-LHC event-reconstruction papers that explicitly cover learned particle-flow reconstruction, pileup/high-occupancy tracking, and detector/physics validation.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"machine learning particle flow reconstruction high luminosity LHC pileup graph neural network tracking validation metrics arXiv","discipline":"physics","max_results":5}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers include Pata et al. “Machine Learning for Particle Flow Reconstruction at CMS” (arXiv:2101.08578), Qasim et al. “Object Condensation: one-stage grid-free multi-object reconstruction...” (arXiv:2002.03605), and Duarte/Exa.TrkX-related graph tracking work such as “Graph Neural Networks for Particle Tracking in High Energy Physics” (arXiv:2003.11603). These cover calorimeter+tracker inputs, truth-particle supervision, pileup robustness, and reconstruction metrics such as particle ID accuracy, energy response/resolution, tracking efficiency, fake rate, and downstream jet/MET performance.  

Rationale: I read the central particle-flow paper because it directly addresses end-to-end event reconstruction from heterogeneous detector elements and is closest to the requested “collision event reconstruction” setting.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:2101.08578","sections":["abstract","method","datasets","loss","results","validation"]}
```  
[result]: The paper trains on simulated CMS events with detector-level inputs such as tracks and calorimeter clusters, supervised by generator/simulation truth particles. It uses permutation-invariant set/graph-style learning for particle-flow candidates and validates with particle multiplicity, particle-type classification, energy/angular response, jet response/resolution, and missing transverse momentum behavior under pileup-like conditions.  

Rationale: I next extract reusable “genome” elements from the reconstruction literature, including physics constraints and limitations, so the final output is structured rather than a narrative summary.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["arXiv:2101.08578","arXiv:2002.03605","arXiv:2003.11603"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanisms include heterogeneous detector-object encoders, graph/message-passing or attention layers, object condensation / set prediction, truth matching, and multi-task losses for ID, momentum, charge, and vertex/pileup association. Common limitations are simulation-to-data domain shift, extreme pileup scalability, detector miscalibration, rare-object coverage, and the need to preserve conservation laws and calibrated uncertainties.  

Rationale: I check novelty against the main candidates to avoid merely restating “use a GNN for reconstruction”; the differentiator should be constraint-aware, data/calibration-aware event reconstruction for high luminosity.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","paper_ids":["arXiv:2101.08578","arXiv:2002.03605","arXiv:2003.11603"],"proposed_angle":"ML-assisted HL-LHC particle-collision event reconstruction with explicit physics constraints, pileup-aware truth supervision, domain-shift calibration, and validation on particle-, object-, and event-level metrics."}
```  
[result]: The angle is partially covered by MLPF/Object Condensation/Exa.TrkX, but a unified gene emphasizing explicit constraints, pileup/vertex disentanglement, uncertainty calibration, and multi-level validation remains a useful synthesis. Novelty is strongest if the proposal couples learned reconstruction with hard/soft conservation constraints and simulation-to-data validation hooks.  

Rationale: I now produce the requested gene_genome, grounded in the retrieved literature and focused on training data, physics constraints, and validation metrics.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Train a heterogeneous event-reconstruction model on detector-level objects from high-luminosity collision simulations: tracker hits/tracks, calorimeter clusters/cells, timing-layer information, muon-system segments, reconstructed vertices, and detector geometry/context. Use graph neural networks, attention/set transformers, or object-condensation-style architectures to map variable-size detector inputs to variable-size particle-flow candidates. Supervision comes from generator/simulation truth particles matched to detector deposits, with multi-task targets for particle identity, four-momentum, charge, production vertex, pileup vs hard-scatter label, and uncertainty estimates. Physics-aware losses should combine classification loss, momentum/energy regression, object-assignment loss, vertex association loss, and optional constraint penalties for charge consistency, mass-shell hypotheses, track-cluster compatibility, momentum balance, and calibrated missing transverse momentum.","niche_genome":"Best suited to HL-LHC and future high-luminosity collider environments where pileup, detector occupancy, and combinatorics make hand-engineered reconstruction difficult. The natural niche is ML-assisted particle-flow/event reconstruction, especially for CMS/ATLAS-like multipurpose detectors and high-granularity calorimeters. Training data should include full detector simulation where available, fast simulation for large statistics, pileup overlays spanning expected luminosity profiles, rare physics samples, calibration/control samples such as Z→μμ, Z→ee, γ+jet, dijet, ttbar, and zero-bias/minimum-bias events, plus realistic detector misalignment, noise, dead channels, and time-dependent conditions.","observation_genome":"Prior work such as MLPF, Object Condensation, and GNN tracking shows that learned set/graph models can reconstruct variable numbers of particles from irregular detector data and can improve or match classical particle-flow components on particle-level and jet-level observables. Key empirical observations are that learned models benefit from local detector geometry, truth matching, pileup labels, and end-to-end optimization against downstream physics quantities; however, validation must be performed not only on per-particle losses but also on reconstructed jets, b-tagging inputs, taus, missing transverse momentum, and benchmark physics distributions.","limitation_genome":"Main risks are simulation-to-data mismatch, insufficient modeling of detector noise/calibration/alignment, poor extrapolation to rare topologies, bias against low-pT or displaced particles, pileup-induced confusion, computational latency under trigger constraints, and lack of transparent uncertainty estimates. Purely learned models may violate exact or approximate physics constraints unless constrained by architecture, loss terms, post-processing, or calibration. Validation also becomes difficult because good average particle loss can still produce biased event-level observables such as MET tails or jet mass shapes.","delta_genome":"Compared with generic ML reconstruction, the high-luminosity version should explicitly encode: pileup-aware vertex/time association; detector-geometry equivariance or locality; track-cluster and charge-conservation consistency; uncertainty calibration; domain adaptation from simulation to data using control samples; and multi-level validation gates before physics use. A strong implementation would train on simulated truth but calibrate/validate on data using tag-and-probe leptons, γ/Z+jet balance, minimum-bias occupancy, pileup profiles, closure tests, and systematic variations of detector conditions.","claim_genome":"A physics-constrained, pileup-aware graph/set reconstruction model trained on simulated high-luminosity detector data and validated on particle-, object-, and event-level metrics can provide more robust particle-flow candidates than purely hand-engineered reconstruction in dense collision environments. Required validation metrics include tracking efficiency and fake/duplicate rate; particle ID accuracy/confusion matrix; energy, pT, η, φ response and resolution; vertex/pileup-association efficiency; jet energy scale/resolution; MET scale, resolution, and tail modeling; lepton/photon/tau efficiencies; b-tagging input stability; computational throughput/latency; calibration closure; uncertainty calibration; and agreement of control-region physics distributions between data and simulation."}}
```

full_prompt: Design a theoretical project on the relationship between chaos, scrambling, and thermalization in many-body quantum systems. Provide idea_plan output with model systems, metrics, and expected insights.
chosen:
Rationale: I first identify canonical evidence anchors spanning quantum chaos bounds, OTOC/scrambling diagnostics, and ETH/thermalization reviews so the project is grounded in established metrics rather than a single model.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"many-body quantum chaos scrambling thermalization ETH OTOC spectral statistics SYK spin chains review","discipline":"physics","max_results":6}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers include Maldacena-Shenker-Stanford on the chaos bound (arXiv:1503.01409), D’Alessio et al. on quantum chaos and ETH (arXiv:1509.06411), Maldacena-Stanford on SYK and maximal chaos (arXiv:1606.01857), Hosur et al. on chaos in quantum channels and OTOCs (arXiv:1511.04021), and Luitz-Laflorencie-Alet on ergodicity breaking/MBL in spin chains (arXiv:1411.0660).  

Rationale: I read the most relevant papers to extract what is known: chaos diagnostics, scrambling diagnostics, and thermalization diagnostics each capture different projections of many-body dynamics.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["1503.01409","1509.06411","1606.01857","1511.04021","1411.0660"],"focus":["chaos metrics","scrambling metrics","ETH thermalization metrics","model systems","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: The literature supports using level statistics/spectral form factors for chaos, OTOCs/operator entanglement for scrambling, and ETH/local relaxation/diagonal-vs-thermal ensembles for thermalization. However, the papers also show these notions need not coincide exactly: integrable systems can spread operators without random-matrix spectra, MBL systems suppress transport/thermalization despite interactions, and SYK-like models scramble maximally but lack spatial hydrodynamics in zero-dimensional form.  

Rationale: I check novelty against likely adjacent projects: “measure OTOCs in a spin chain,” “compare ETH and level statistics,” and “study SYK chaos” are not novel alone, so the contribution must be a unified map of when implications between chaos, scrambling, and thermalization fail.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","paper_ids":["1503.01409","1509.06411","1606.01857","1511.04021","1411.0660"],"proposed_claim":"Construct a comparative theoretical phase diagram across SYK, nonintegrable spin chains, integrable chains, MBL chains, and Floquet/prethermal systems, quantifying implication gaps among spectral chaos, spatial scrambling, and ETH thermalization using matched finite-size and time-window scaling."}
```  
[result]: Novelty is plausible if framed as an implication-structure project rather than another OTOC computation: the distinctive element is a common diagnostic pipeline and a classification of regimes where chaos ⇒ scrambling ⇒ thermalization, or where one implication fails. The main risk is finite-size ambiguity, so the proposal should emphasize scaling collapses, cross-model robustness, and diagnostic consistency checks.  

Rationale: I now propose a theoretical project with explicit model systems, metrics, implementation order, and expected insights in the requested `idea_plan` schema.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","idea_plan":{"Idea":"Develop a unified theoretical framework for the relationship between quantum chaos, scrambling, and thermalization by comparing several many-body models with identical diagnostics. The central hypothesis is that these three concepts form overlapping but non-equivalent dynamical properties: spectral chaos is closely tied to ETH thermalization in closed nonintegrable systems, scrambling diagnoses operator growth and information delocalization, and thermalization requires additional ingredients such as dephasing, transport, and absence of extensive conserved quantities. The project will identify regimes where the implications chaos -> scrambling -> thermalization hold and regimes where they fail.","ImplementationSteps":{"1_ModelSystems":"Study a controlled hierarchy of models: (i) SYK q=4 model as a benchmark for fast all-to-all scrambling and near-maximal Lyapunov growth; (ii) tilted-field nonintegrable Ising or XXZ spin chain as a spatially local chaotic thermalizer; (iii) integrable XXZ/free-fermion limits as cases with ballistic spreading but constrained thermalization to a generalized Gibbs ensemble; (iv) disordered Heisenberg chain across ergodic-to-MBL crossover to separate interaction-induced dephasing from thermalization failure; and (v) periodically driven Floquet spin chains to test heating, prethermal plateaus, and random-matrix spectral statistics without energy conservation.","2_ChaosDiagnostics":"Compute adjacent gap ratio <r>, full level-spacing distributions, spectral form factor K(t), eigenvector participation/complexity, and random-matrix ensemble crossover. Use symmetry-resolved spectra to avoid false chaos signals.","3_ScramblingDiagnostics":"Compute infinite- and finite-temperature OTOCs C_ij(t)=-<[W_i(t),V_j]^2>, extract Lyapunov exponent lambda_L where meaningful, butterfly velocity v_B in spatial systems, operator entanglement growth, Pauli-string/operator-size distributions, and tripartite mutual information for information delocalization.","4_ThermalizationDiagnostics":"Test ETH via eigenstate expectation value smoothness and off-diagonal ETH scaling; simulate quenches and compare local observables with microcanonical/canonical predictions; compute entanglement entropy growth and saturation; measure diagonal-ensemble vs thermal error; and, for integrable cases, compare with generalized Gibbs ensemble predictions.","5_TheorySynthesis":"Construct an implication map using dimensionless indices: chaos index from spectral statistics, scrambling index from OTOC/operator-size growth, and thermalization index from ETH/quench errors. Analyze finite-size scaling and time-window dependence to distinguish true asymptotic behavior from transient or prethermal behavior."},"ImplementationOrder":["Define symmetry sectors, parameter windows.","Build cross-model phase diagrams comparing chaos index, scrambling index, and thermalization index.","Identify counterexample regimes and formulate theoretical criteria for when one dimension","off-diagonal ETH scaling","long-time local-observable error relative to microcanonical/canonical ensemble","diagonal ensemble vs thermal ensemble trace-distance proxy on local subsystems","entanglement entropy saturation near Page/thermal value","transport coefficients or diffusion constants when spatially resolved"],"CrossRelation":["correlation matrix among chaos, scrambling, and thermalization indices","finite-size scaling of mismatched regimes","classification accuracy of predicting thermalization from chaos-plus-scrambling features","identification of counterexamples: integrable scrambling without ETH, MBL interaction without thermalization, Floquet heating with distinct conservation laws"]},"ExpectedOutcome":"The project should yield a theory-level phase diagram showing that chaos, scrambling, and thermalization are related but not synonymous. Expected insights are: (i) nonintegrable local systems typically show aligned random-matrix spectra, ballistic operator growth, and ETH thermalization after the Thouless time; (ii) SYK gives strong evidence that rapid scrambling can occur without conventional spatial transport; (iii) integrable systems can show operator spreading and entanglement growth while failing ordinary ETH, thermalizing instead to a GGE; (iv) MBL systems can have slow logarithmic entanglement growth and residual local memory, breaking the scrambling-to-thermalization link; and (v) Floquet/prethermal systems reveal that spectral chaos may coexist with long-lived nonthermal plateaus. The final theoretical product is a set of conditional criteria for when chaos is a reliable predictor of scrambling and thermalization, and when additional conservation laws, locality, disorder, or prethermal constraints invalidate that inference."}}
```  

full_prompt: Propose a study of acoustic analogs of event horizons in flowing condensates. Provide idea_plan output including setup, measurable correlations, and theoretical interpretation.
chosen:
Rationale: Start from landmark experimental and theoretical work on sonic horizons in Bose–Einstein condensates to anchor the proposal in demonstrated observables and known gaps.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"acoustic black hole Bose Einstein condensate Hawking radiation density-density correlations experiment theory","discipline":"physics","max_results":5}
```  
[result]: Found core candidates: Lahav et al., “Realization of a Sonic Black Hole Analog in a Bose-Einstein Condensate,” PRL 105, 240401 (2010), DOI:10.1103/PhysRevLett.105.240401; Carusotto et al., “Numerical observation of Hawking radiation from acoustic black holes in atomic Bose-Einstein condensates,” NJP 10, 103001 (2008), DOI:10.1088/1367-2630/10/10/103001; Steinhauer, “Observation of quantum Hawking radiation and its entanglement in an analogue black hole,” Nat. Phys. 12, 959–965 (2016), DOI:10.1038/nphys3863; de Nova et al., “Observation of thermal Hawking radiation and its temperature in an analogue black hole,” Nature 569, 688–691 (2019), DOI:10.1038/s41586-019-1241-0.  

Rationale: Read the two most relevant empirical papers to identify the concrete setup, measured correlations, and interpretive assumptions that a new study should extend rather than repeat.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1038/nphys3863","10.1038/s41586-019-1241-0"],"sections":["abstract","methods","results","figures"]}
```  
[result]: The 2016 work reports density-density correlation bands across a BEC sonic horizon and infers entangled Hawking/partner phonon pairs via momentum-space correlations. The 2019 work emphasizes the thermal spectrum and Hawking temperature scaling with surface gravity, using repeated in situ density profiles of a quasi-1D flowing condensate with subsonic/supersonic regions.  

Rationale: Extract the “genome” of the existing approach so the proposed study has an explicit delta: what mechanism, niche, observation, and limitation it targets.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.1103/PhysRevLett.105.240401","10.1038/nphys3863","10.1038/s41586-019-1241-0"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: mechanism_genome: transonic BEC flow creates an acoustic metric with a sonic horizon and Bogoliubov mode conversion; niche_genome: quasi-1D ultracold atomic condensates with engineered potential steps; observation_genome: equal-time density-density and momentum correlations revealing Hawking/partner phonon pairs and approximate thermality; limitation_genome: finite-size/finite-time effects, stimulation by technical noise, limited access to full frequency-resolved entanglement, and weak tests of dispersive corrections; delta_genome: combine horizon modulation, frequency-resolved Bragg readout, and controlled initial phonon seeding; claim_genome: distinguish spontaneous Hawking emission from stimulated emission while testing dispersive acoustic-metric predictions.
